summary: Survey mainstream LDO/supply modeling methods and compare to our per-block behavioral model

findings.category: Analytical / Classical Small-Signal LDO Models (hand-derived symbolic transfer functions, pole-zero placement, loop-gain/PM, closed-form PSRR and Zout, output-noise derivations)

methods.name: Rincon-Mora / Gupta voltage-divider (impedance-ratio) PSRR model
methods.what: The canonical hand-model for LDO power-supply rejection. The supply ripple vin reaches vout through a voltage divider: the series impedance from VIN to VOUT is the pass-device output resistance roP (PMOS rds, or for an NMOS source-follower roughly 1/gm), and the shunt impedance to ground is the parallel combination of the open-loop output filter ZO (RL || 1/(sCO) including RESR, plus RFB1+RFB2) and the closed-loop shunt impedance ZO-REG = (ZO||roP)/LGV that the feedback loop creates. Supply gain AIN = vout/vin = (ZO||ZO-REG)/(roP + ZO||ZO-REG), and PSR = 1/AIN. In closed form the PSRR Bode plot has PSRdc = (Aol*beta)^-1 (set by DC loop gain), a dominant ZERO at z1 = BWA (error-amp/dominant-pole bandwidth, where loop gain starts dropping), a pole p1 = UGF (loop unity-gain frequency, where PSR is worst, typically 0.1-10 MHz), a pole p2 = pout (the output pole when CO shunts roP), and an ESR zero z2 = 1/(2*pi*RESR*CO) that degrades high-frequency rejection. The whole approach reuses op-amp PSRR theory (Steyaert-Sansen, Sackinger).
methods.where_used: Georgia Tech (Gabriel Rincon-Mora, Vishal Gupta, Amit Patel) JSSC/TCAS-II/ISCAS/SOC papers and Rincon-Mora's book 'Analog IC Design with Low-Dropout Regulators' (McGraw-Hill, 2009). It is the standard mental model taught in industry/academia for LDO PSRR and is the basis of most app-note PSRR explanations (TI, ADI, Maxim AN-883/898).
strengths: Gives direct physical insight: PSR = ratio of supply-to-output impedance over output-to-ground impedance; you immediately see what raises PSR (raise roP / supply impedance, lower ZO-REG / raise loop gain, add Cout).

Closed-form PSRR(f) with explicit DC value and pole/zero list (PSRdc, BWA, UGF, pout, ESR zero) maps one-to-one onto the measured PSRR curve and the 'worst-case PSR near UGF' notch that matters for RF supplies.
Correctly predicts the high-frequency floor set by CO and RESR (PSR -> roP/RESR), and the distinction between PMOS, NMOS-cascode, NMOS source-follower supply paths.
Calculated vs simulated pole/zero agreement is good (their Table I: fZD/fCO/fPD/fO/fEA within ~10-15%), so it is genuinely predictive, not just qualitative.
weaknesses: Single-input model: classically derived for the VIN->VOUT path only. To use it for spur fidelity you must separately and self-consistently fit Zout(f) and the loop gain; the same roP/loop-gain quantities appear in both, and hand algebra rarely keeps them phase-consistent.

Pole-zero counting is approximate (widely-separated-pole assumption). Near a Zout/loop resonance (complex pole pair, peaking/inductive bump) the real-pole cascade misrepresents both magnitude AND phase, which is exactly where transient ringing and spur phase live.

Captures only deterministic small-signal ripple transfer. It says nothing about discrete spurs originating inside the LDO (charge-pump ripple, reference/clock tones, bias spurs) - those are added by hand as separate sources, not predicted.
Assumes LTI, single bias point; gm-compression, slewing, and the load-dependence of roP/pout (PSR shifts with IL) require re-deriving per operating point.

Bipolar/PNP and current-mode variants (Patel-Rincon-Mora) add a second series-feedback loop and several more poles/zeros, so the 'simple divider' becomes a multi-loop algebra exercise that is easy to get wrong.
methods.supply_handling: This is THE classical method for Zout(f) and PSRR(f) as closed forms: ZO-REG = (ZO||roP)/LGV makes the loop-gain-shaped output impedance explicit, and PSR = 1/AIN with poles at BWA/UGF/pout and the ESR zero. It does NOT natively produce noise PSD (added as separate output-referred sources) and does NOT produce discrete spurs (must be injected by hand). For PSS/HB it is not a runnable model by itself - it is a recipe whose extracted poles/zeros you then realize as a laplace_nd/Foster network; if you keep magnitude AND phase of Zout and the VIN->VOUT path consistent it feeds spur fidelity well, but the real-pole approximations near resonances are the weak point for spur amplitude/phase accuracy.

methods.name: Loop-gain / phase-margin closed-loop Zout reduction (feedback theory: Zout = Zo,ol/(1+T(s)))
methods.what: The classical feedback view (Razavi 'The Low Dropout Regulator: A Circuit for All Seasons', IEEE SSC-M 2019; ADI 'Ask The Applications Engineer-37'). Open-loop output impedance Zo,ol (set by pass-device output resistance in parallel with RL, gds3, R1+R2 and CO) is reduced by the return ratio: Zout(s) = Zo,ol(s)/(1+T(s)), where T(s) is the loop gain (error-amp gain * pass-device gm * output network). At DC, Zout is tiny (Zo,ol/T0). As frequency rises, T(s) rolls off at -20 dB/dec past the dominant pole, so Zout RISES with frequency at +20 dB/dec - i.e. the LDO looks INDUCTIVE - until CO takes over and Zout falls again. This inductive region, interacting with load capacitance, forms a series-RLC and is the source of the transient ringing and the Zout 'bump'. Loop gain T(s), phase margin, dominant pole (CO*RL or Miller), gain-bandwidth and the ESR zero (placed to recover phase margin) are the design knobs.
methods.where_used: Razavi (UCLA) tutorial paper; ADI Analog Dialogue (Ask The Applications Engineer-37, Understand LDO Concepts); standard in every analog-IC textbook (Razavi 'Design of Analog CMOS ICs', Sansen). Universally used by designers to reason about stability and transient response before any simulation.
strengths: Directly connects stability (phase margin of T(s)) to output-impedance shape and to transient overshoot/ringing - one transfer function explains AC, transient and PSRR behavior.
The Zout = Zo,ol/(1+T) form is exact in the LTI sense and exposes the inductive-rise mechanism, which is precisely the resonant Zout feature your project found governs the load-step ring.

Clear pass-device contrast: PMOS common-source vs NMOS source-follower change Zo,ol, the location of the output pole, and the supply path, so the same framework explains why NMOS LDOs have ~20-30 dB better PSRR.
Easy to convert into a behavioral model: realize T(s) and Zo,ol as rational functions, then Zout follows; this is the cleanest path to a phase-correct Zout for PSS/HB.
weaknesses: Requires an accurate symbolic T(s); for multi-stage / nested-Miller / DFCFC capless loops the hand-derived T(s) gets high-order and error-prone, and the '1+T' division can hide where complex poles actually land.

The inductive-Zout / complex-pole region is exactly where the dominant-pole approximation is least valid, so hand numbers for the resonant peak Q and frequency are often off - and that peak sets ring amplitude/spur sidebands.
Says nothing about noise or spurs directly - those are bolted on.
Load-, ESR- and Cout-dependence means a single T(s) is only valid at one operating corner; capless vs large-Cout designs need different dominant-pole stories.
methods.supply_handling: Best classical handle on Zout(f) WITH correct phase (the +20 dB/dec inductive rise and the CO-induced fall), because Zout = Zo,ol/(1+T) preserves phase if T(s) is rational. PSRR can be derived in the same framework (forward supply path / (1+T)). Noise PSD and discrete spurs are out of scope. For PSS/HB: this is the most convergence-friendly basis - realize T(s) and Zo,ol as low-order rational laplace_nd blocks and the LDO becomes a passive, smooth LTI two-port (Zout + PSRR), which HB/shooting handle with no Newton-convergence trouble; the only fidelity risk is under-modeling the resonant pole pair.

methods.name: Closed-form output-noise PSD decomposition (input-referred noise * noise gain)
methods.what: Analytical LDO output-noise model: refer every internal noise source to the error-amp input, then multiply by the closed-loop noise gain (1 + R1/R2) up to the loop bandwidth. En_out = sqrt(En_div^2 + En_ref^2 + En_erramp^2): contributions from the feedback divider, the voltage reference (bandgap), and the error amplifier. The reference and error-amp dominate; the pass FET is negligible because its noise is divided by the large open-loop gain between it and the EA input (TI SLAA412 'LDO Noise Demystified'; ADI AN-1120). Each source carries a 1/f (flicker) part rising at low f and a flat thermal floor; the loop low-pass-shapes them and the bandgap is RC-filtered (large internal R + external Cnr) so the noise rolls off above the loop/filter bandwidth. Output noise is specified either as spectral density (uV/rtHz) - needed for RF/sideband work - or integrated RMS (uVrms) - for sampled systems.
methods.where_used: TI app notes (SLAA412), ADI AN-1120 'Noise Sources in Low Dropout Regulators', ultra-low-noise LDO datasheets (ADM717x, TPS717xx, LT306x), and academic low-1/f-noise LDO papers (MDPI Electronics 2025). Standard for noise budgeting.
strengths: Identifies the right dominant sources (reference + EA) and the noise-gain (1+R1/R2) multiplier, giving a quick uVrms/uV-rtHz estimate from a few parameters.
Explains the two real reduction levers (lower EA bandwidth; RC-filter the bandgap with a noise-reduction cap) that designers actually use.
Naturally separates flicker (1/f) and thermal (white) regions, matching measured spectral-density curves; gives the spectral shape RF sideband analysis needs.
weaknesses: Classical version is white+1/f only and assumes noise rides 'through' a flat gain; it ignores that the output noise is LOOP-SHAPED by Zout (your finding: Sout = Sv*|Yout|^2 + PSRR path), so a naive white-through-flat-gain model mis-shapes the spectrum near the Zout resonance.
Predicts a smooth PSD - it does NOT predict the DISCRETE spurs that real LDOs emit (reference/clock/charge-pump tones); those must be added as deterministic sources.
Source values (kf flicker coeff, reference noise) are process-specific fit parameters, not derivable by hand; the model is a template you must calibrate.
Integrated-RMS number loses frequency info and is useless for placing a sideband relative to an RF carrier.
methods.supply_handling: Gives the output NOISE PSD as a closed form (sum of referred sources * noise gain, with 1/f + thermal shape and a loop/filter low-pass). Properly extended it becomes an output-referred shaped Norton source (In = Sv/|Zout|) plus the PSRR path - the generalization your project adopted. It does NOT model discrete spurs (a known gap that must be filled by an explicit tone source) and is silent on Zout/PSRR phase. For PSS/HB: noise is handled in the separate Pnoise/Hbnoise step, where Spectre folds noise sidebands via periodic transfer functions (PXF) to the carrier; a behavioral shaped-Norton + PSRR noise model plugs straight into that and converges trivially since it is linear, but its spur-fidelity value is zero unless deterministic tones are added on top.

methods.name: Capless / multi-stage symbolic compensation models (DFCFC, nested-Miller, pole-splitting)
methods.what: For internally-compensated (capacitor-less) LDOs the output pole is no longer dominant, so designers hand-derive a 3-stage-amplifier transfer function and apply pole-splitting / nested-Miller / damping-factor-control frequency compensation (Leung & Mok, JSSC 2003) to place a complex-conjugate non-dominant pole pair with a controlled damping factor and to track the pole that moves with load current. The closed-form loop gain becomes a 3rd/4th-order rational with deliberately positioned zeros (Miller, feedforward CFF) cancelling parasitic poles, and Zout/PSRR are derived from that higher-order T(s).
methods.where_used: Leung-Mok (HKUST) and successors; capless LDOs for SoC point-of-load; MIT/GIST low-noise three-stage LDO theses; modern fast-transient capless designs. Used wherever no off-chip Cout is allowed.
strengths: Handles the regime your generalization work hit (capless, multi-pole) where the simple single-output-pole divider model breaks.
Explicitly models the complex-pole pair and its damping factor - i.e. the resonant Zout peak - rather than approximating it with real poles, so it can predict ring Q and the spur-relevant peaking.
Provides principled zero placement (feedforward/Miller) that you can replicate in a behavioral Zout/PSRR fit.
weaknesses: High-order symbolic algebra (3-4 poles, multiple zeros, load-dependent) is tedious and error-prone by hand; most designers fall back to simulation to confirm.
Strongly load- and process-dependent; the pole pair migrates with IL and gm, so a single closed form is only locally valid.
Still LTI/small-signal: no spurs, no large-signal slew, no native noise.
Damping-factor expressions are sensitive to parasitic estimates that are hard to know without extraction.
methods.supply_handling: Produces a higher-order, complex-pole Zout(f) and PSRR(f) that more faithfully captures resonant peaking/inductive behavior than the real-pole divider model - good for phase-correct Zout. Noise and spurs still external. For PSS/HB: realizing the 3rd/4th-order T(s) and the resulting Zout as a rational laplace_nd block is still smooth and convergence-friendly; the higher order slightly raises HB harmonic/sideband bookkeeping but remains far cheaper and more robust than the transistor-level multistage loop, which itself can struggle with PSS shooting convergence.

methods.name: NMOS source-follower vs PMOS common-source pass-device supply-path analysis
methods.what: Classical comparison of how the pass device sets the VIN->VOUT supply path and thus PSRR. A PMOS common-source pass device couples supply ripple through its gate-referenced gain (and its rds), giving moderate PSRR that the loop must fight. An NMOS source-follower pass device intrinsically attenuates supply ripple (source follows gate, and the gate is regulated/clean), so VIN->VOUT is ~1/gm-divided rather than rds-divided, yielding ~20-30 dB better PSRR below and above the dominant pole - at the cost of a gate drive above VIN (charge pump) and headroom. Hand analysis expresses the supply path roP (PMOS) vs ~1/gm and the follower's ripple-replica behavior at the source.
methods.where_used: Razavi tutorial; multiple JSSC/patent NMOS-LDO and super-source-follower works; supply-noise-cancellation NMOS LDO patents (US9577508); RF/PLL/VCO point-of-load LDOs where PSRR is paramount.
strengths: Explains the single biggest architectural PSRR lever (NMOS-SF >> PMOS) in closed form, directly relevant to choosing/representing the supply element near an RF carrier.
Maps cleanly to the divider model's roP term (just swap roP for the follower's ~1/gm supply impedance).
Captures the 'follower passes a replica of gate ripple' subtlety that determines residual PSRR.
weaknesses: Qualitative-to-semi-quantitative; exact numbers need the EA PSRR and charge-pump ripple folded in.
NMOS-SF introduces charge-pump switching spurs onto the rail - a DISCRETE-spur source the small-signal analysis ignores.
Headroom/charge-pump complexity means the 'clean gate' assumption can fail and re-inject supply ripple.
methods.supply_handling: Sets the DC/HF asymptotes of the VIN->VOUT (PSRR) path and the supply-side term of Zout. Directly informs the behavioral PSRR block's high-frequency floor. It actually FLAGS a discrete-spur source (charge-pump ripple in NMOS-SF designs) that pure small-signal models miss - useful as a reminder to add a deterministic tone at the pump frequency. For PSS/HB the choice only changes the fitted PSRR(f) asymptotes, not the convergence behavior of a behavioral two-port.

methods.name: Realization of hand-derived transfer functions as Verilog-A/Laplace behavioral two-ports (the bridge to PSS/HB)
methods.what: Not a derivation method but the standard way classical results are USED in EDA: take the hand-derived Zout(s), PSRR(s)=VIN->VOUT(s), and noise PSD, and realize them as a behavioral two-port - either pole-zero Laplace primitives (Verilog-A laplace_zp/laplace_np/laplace_nd, Spectre ahdlLib) or an equivalent Foster/Cauer RLC ladder - presenting the supply node as an LTI element (regulated source + series Zout + ripple-injection via PSRR), with deterministic spur tones added as ideal sources and noise as a shaped Norton source.
methods.where_used: Cadence Spectre/SpectreRF behavioral flows (ahdlLib, Verilog-A), the EDABoard/Cadence-forum recommended LDO behavioral-modeling practice (model the op-amp/loop via laplace_zp), Qucs/HB behavioral-macromodel literature (Brinson). This is exactly the family your project's emitted SPICE/Verilog-A model lives in.
strengths: Turns paper transfer functions into a runnable model that is orders of magnitude faster than transistor-level in PSS/HB and avoids the regulator loop's Newton-convergence pitfalls.
If Zout and PSRR are kept rational with correct magnitude AND phase, the model reproduces transient ringing, PSRR notch, and carrier-sideband ripple faithfully.
Linear LTI blocks partition cleanly in HB (Brinson: keep nonlinear parts in Verilog-A, linear parts as components, one linear element per macromodel node) - excellent convergence.
Deterministic spur tones and shaped-Norton noise can be superposed without disturbing the LTI core, giving full spur fidelity in one model.
weaknesses: Pure-Laplace blocks can have convergence/causality issues in shooting/transient if poles are near-imaginary or if a non-minimum-phase response is forced through a minimum-phase realization (your V4 PSRR non-min-phase fix is exactly this trap).
Trapezoidal-rule ringing in PSS shooting can stall on high-Q rational blocks; needs gear2/trap method and adequate tstab.
Fidelity is only as good as the hand-extracted poles/zeros - garbage Q/phase in gives garbage spur sidebands out.
High-order Laplace blocks increase HB harmonic/sideband count and memory; must keep order minimal.
methods.supply_handling: This is where Zout(f), PSRR(f), noise PSD, AND discrete spurs are finally COMBINED into one supply element: series Zout (rational), VIN->VOUT PSRR path (rational), shaped-Norton noise (for Pnoise/Hbnoise PXF folding to the carrier), and explicit tone sources for deterministic spurs. Done right it is the fastest, most convergent route to PSS/HB spur fidelity; the failure modes are non-minimum-phase mis-realization, too-high-Q rational poles stalling shooting, and forgetting that spurs must be explicit (the LTI core never creates them).

findings.relevance_to_spur_pss: Highly relevant as the FOUNDATION, with sharp limits. The analytical/classical family is exactly what lets you replace a transistor-level LDO with a fast, convergent supply element for PSS/HB near an RF carrier: (1) The loop-gain / closed-loop-output-impedance result Zout(s)=Zo,ol/(1+T(s)) and the Rincon-Mora voltage-divider PSRR (PSRdc=(Aol*beta)^-1, dominant zero at BWA, pole at UGF, output pole pout, ESR zero at 1/2*pi*RESR*CO) give you closed-form Zout(f) and PSRR(f) that, when realized as low-order rational laplace_nd/Foster two-ports with correct MAGNITUDE AND PHASE, reproduce both the transient ring and the carrier-sideband ripple while converging far better in shooting/HB than the real regulator loop (no Newton-stall on the multistage loop, smooth LTI partitioning per Brinson). This matches your project's findings that the spur band is linear and that transient is governed by a phase-correct Zout. (2) The classical noise decomposition gives the output noise PSD shape (1/f + thermal, noise-gain (1+R1/R2)) that, once loop/Zout-shaped into a Norton source, folds correctly to carrier sidebands via Spectre Pnoise/Hbnoise PXF. BUT three things the classical small-signal family fundamentally CANNOT do and that dominate spur fidelity: (a) it never predicts DISCRETE SPURS - reference/clock/charge-pump/bias tones must be added as explicit deterministic sources at vout (your spur block); the LTI core creates no new tones. (b) Its widely-separated-real-pole approximations misrepresent the resonant/complex-pole Zout peak and the non-minimum-phase PSRR - exactly the magnitude/phase region that sets spur amplitude and phase (your V4 non-min-phase fix and the need for signed Foster sections). (c) It is single-bias-point LTI, so gm-compression/slew and load-dependent pole migration are out of scope (fine here since your spur band is linear, but a caveat). Net: use the classical results to STRUCTURE and seed the behavioral Zout/PSRR/noise blocks (right poles/zeros, right asymptotes, right physical knobs), then fit magnitude+phase to ground-truth and bolt on explicit spur tones - which is precisely the per-block swappable model your memory describes.

findings.category: Behavioral macromodeling in Verilog-A / Verilog-AMS / VHDL-AMS and controlled-source equivalent-circuit macromodels for LDOs/regulators/op-amps

methods.name: Controlled-source equivalent-circuit op-amp/regulator macromodel (Boyle-style and modern G/E-element)
methods.what: The classic SPICE-netlist macromodel: replace the transistor-level circuit with a small fixed topology of controlled sources (E=VCVS, F=CCCS, G=VCCS, H=CCVS), R, C, L, and diodes that reproduces the terminal behavior. Boyle/Cohn/Pederson/Solomon (1974) and Alexander/Bowers established the canonical three-stage form: a (gain-normalized) input differential stage, an intermediate gain+pole-shaping stage, and an output stage with current/voltage limiters and a slew-rate clamp. Modern versions (e.g., Renesas SLG88103V whitepaper, 2017/2025) keep the cascaded-section idea but drop the input transistor pair entirely, building the input stage from diodes + dependent sources so that CMRR, PSRR+, PSRR-, Vos, bias/offset current, voltage/current noise and their temperature dependence are each modeled by a dedicated G-R-L-E sub-ladder. Pole/zero shaping is done with explicit RC stages (R23/C4 second pole, R24/C5 dominant pole, extra stages added as needed); slew rate via reverse-breakdown diodes (BV=SR*C); output impedance via G14/R33 + L6 networks.
methods.where_used: The dominant industry approach for vendor SPICE models distributed with parts (ADI/LTspice, TI, Renesas/Dialog, Microchip, ON Semi). Runs in any SPICE-class engine: Spectre, HSPICE, Eldo/Questa-ADMS, PSpice, LTspice, ngspice, AFS/Spectre APS. Used for board/PMIC/SoC system simulation where the designer needs a part-accurate but cheap supply/amplifier model.
strengths: Engine-agnostic and ubiquitous — runs everywhere SPICE runs; no Verilog-A compiler needed
Each electrical parameter (PSRR, CMRR, noise, Zout, slew, Iq) is an independently tunable sub-network, so datasheet fitting is direct and modular
Built from real R/C/L/diode/controlled-source primitives, so it is inherently charge/current-based and natural state lives in caps/inductors — generally PSS/HB friendly (no hidden state)
Renesas reports good speedup vs transistor-level with measured-vs-simulated agreement on gain/phase, CMRR, PSRR, noise PSD, step response
Mature: 50 years of accumulated practice, well-understood failure modes
weaknesses: Hand-built topology is laborious and brittle; adding poles/zeros or a second Zout resonance means editing the netlist by hand
Boyle-style gain-normalized input stage does NOT represent the true input/supply path, so PSRR, CMRR, Zout are often crude or DC-only in legacy models — the well-known weak spot
Diode-based slew/limit blocks introduce hard nonlinearities (clamps) that hurt HB/PSS convergence if they are active at the period boundary

Discrete-spur generation is not native: a controlled-source linear macromodel only passes a supply tone to the output through its PSRR/Zout path — it will reproduce a spur if the supply stimulus contains it, but it does not itself synthesize deterministic tones
Noise is modeled as PSD sources (white R + 1/f diode) for .noise analysis; these do not become time-domain spurs and are excluded from PSS large-signal solution
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): modeled explicitly with G/R/L output ladders (Renesas G14/R33/L6) — can give a resonant Zout if an L and C are included; only as accurate as the hand-placed poles. PSRR(f): modeled with dedicated supply-sensing sub-ladders (G4-R15-L2-R16-E6 for PSRR+, G5-R17-L3-R18-E8 for PSRR-) where the L sets the +20dB/dec rolloff — gives a real frequency-dependent PSRR injected into the signal path, working in both AC and transient. Noise PSD: white via a resistor, 1/f via series diode+source; valid only in .noise/.AC. Discrete spurs: only reproduced if the supply node is driven with the tone; the model multiplies it by PSRR(f) — fidelity of the sideband equals fidelity of the fitted PSRR/Zout. PSS/HB: because state is in real caps/inductors (ddt-equivalent), these models are usually accepted by shooting and HB; the risk is the diode clamps (slew/output limiters) being active at a shooting boundary, causing non-convergence — mitigated by setting tstab so clamps are inactive at the boundary.

methods.name: Verilog-A behavioral macromodel using Laplace operators (laplace_nd / laplace_zp / laplace_zd / laplace_np)
methods.what: Write the regulator/op-amp as a Verilog-A module whose transfer characteristics (open-loop gain, Zout, PSRR, line/load reg) are expressed as rational transfer functions via the built-in Laplace filter operators. The error amp is an ideal high-gain block with a laplace_nd loop-gain shape; the pass device and feedback set DC line/load regulation; PSRR is a second laplace_nd whose input is the sensed supply node V(vdd) and whose output is summed (via a contribution) into V(vout); Zout is realized either as an explicit Norton current contribution through a laplace_nd admittance or as the closed-loop result of the modeled loop gain. This is the form taught in Kundert's Designer's Guide to Verilog-AMS and in university Verilog-A courses (Saxena ECE504), and the form the LDO chapters (Springer/MDPI) use.
methods.where_used: Cadence Spectre/AMS Designer, Synopsys (CustomSim/HSPICE Verilog-A), Siemens Eldo/Questa, Keysight ADS (Verilog-A for HB). Used by IP/PMIC teams for SoC-level co-simulation and by the automotive LDO PSRR-modeling work (MDPI Mathematics 2022). It is the family the user's own project uses (laplace-based PSRR/Zout blocks).
strengths: Compact and parametric: a full PSRR(f) or Zout(f) is one laplace_nd numerator/denominator coefficient list — trivial to refit from extracted poles/zeros

laplace_nd state is a SpectreRF-SUPPORTED differential-operator state (Kundert: ddt, idt, idtmod and Laplace filter functions are the only state SpectreRF handles natively) — so it is legal and convergent in PSS shooting and HB, unlike delay/z-domain forms
Exact small-signal frequency response in AC and correct linear propagation in PSS/HB small-signal phases, so a supply tone is passed to the output as a true sideband with correct magnitude AND phase
Separable per-effect blocks (loop gain, PSRR+, PSRR-, Zout, noise) map cleanly onto the physics
Can add white_noise()/flicker_noise() contributions that feed .noise and pnoise analyses
weaknesses: High-order laplace_nd can be numerically stiff; Cadence's Andrew Beckett notes that naively replacing laplace_nd with an explicit ddt-based ODE (tracking 3rd derivatives) causes 'no convergence at minimum time step' — the Laplace operator is better-conditioned, but very high order or widely-spaced poles still stress the solver

Laplace operators are linear/time-invariant: a single laplace_nd cannot create new tones; nonlinear spur generation (e.g., supply-dependent gm modulation, mixing) must be added explicitly with smooth analog expressions, and those must be C-infinity to stay HB-convergent
Transient accuracy of Laplace operators depends on simulator's internal convolution/recursive-filter implementation and timestep; impulse-response truncation can smear fast Zout ringing

Getting both magnitude AND phase of PSRR/Zout right requires a true (possibly non-minimum-phase) fit — minimum-phase-only fits give wrong transient/sideband phase (a boundary the user's project already hit)
Authoring still requires Verilog-A toolchain and per-effect fitting; not as plug-and-play as a vendor .lib
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): a laplace_nd admittance/impedance driven by the load gives an exact, phase-correct Zout including resonances — strong. PSRR(f): a dedicated laplace_nd from V(vdd) to a vout contribution gives exact frequency-dependent supply rejection in AC, transient, PSS and HB; this is the cleanest way to inject a supply ripple as an output sideband. Noise PSD: white_noise()/flicker_noise() contributions give correct output noise PSD for .noise/pnoise; for loop-shaped noise the Norton-at-vout (Sin=Sv*|Yout|^2) formulation is needed (matches the user's finding). Discrete spurs: a pure-Laplace model is linear so it only RELAYS supply spurs through PSRR(f) — fidelity of the sideband = fidelity of the laplace fit; deterministic self-generated tones require an explicit smooth nonlinear contribution. PSS/HB: best-in-class among behavioral options because Laplace state is exactly the state SpectreRF/HB knows how to set/read/sensitize; no hidden state, co-periodic with sources, robust under shooting and harmonic balance.

methods.name: Behavioral macromodel using lumped-RC / ddt-idt ODE realizations (no Laplace operator)
methods.what: Instead of laplace_nd, realize the same poles/zeros with explicit reactive contributions: I(n) <+ C*ddt(V(n)), V(n) <+ L*ddt(I(n)), or integral forms with idt/idtmod, optionally building the transfer function as a state-space/ODE in Verilog-A. PSRR and Zout become small explicit RLC sub-networks (the Verilog-A analogue of the controlled-source macromodel). This is the bridge between the netlist macromodel and the Laplace-operator model.
methods.where_used: Used when designers want explicit, physically-interpretable state (e.g., to expose an output cap, ESR, bondwire L), when the simulator's Laplace implementation is suspected, or in engines with weak Laplace support. Common in Verilog-AMS power-converter models (Spiazzi/Buso) and in compact-model practice generally.
strengths: State lives in ddt/idt/idtmod operators, which Kundert confirms are SpectreRF-supported — so these models are PSS/HB-legal when written carefully
Physically interpretable: each pole is a real C or L you can tie to a layout parasitic (Cout, ESR, bondwire) — good for what-if and for matching transient ringing exactly
Can be made fully charge-based (q=C*V), which is the most convergence-robust formulation for Newton/shooting
No dependence on the simulator's Laplace convolution engine
weaknesses: Andrew Beckett (Cadence forum) documents the central pitfall: an explicit ddt-ODE that is the algebraic equivalent of a laplace_nd can FAIL transient convergence ('No convergence with minimum time step') where the Laplace operator converged — high derivatives are numerically sensitive and need rescaling and custom natures/disciplines with their own abstol/blowup
More verbose and error-prone than one laplace_nd line for high-order responses

Easy to accidentally create hidden state (a variable used before set, or set only inside @cross/@timer) which SpectreRF outlaws and aborts on — must keep every variable assigned every iteration (Kundert)
Same linearity limitation: no native spur synthesis
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): exact and phase-correct if the RLC ladder matches the extracted impedance; naturally captures resonant ringing in transient. PSRR(f): an explicit RLC supply-to-output network injects the ripple with correct frequency shaping (same idea as the controlled-source macromodel, but in Verilog-A). Noise PSD: add white_noise/flicker_noise contributions. Discrete spurs: linear — relays supply tones only. PSS/HB: supported because state is in ddt/idt/idtmod, BUT more fragile than laplace_nd — high-order ddt forms can blow up minimum timestep (Beckett), and careless coding introduces hidden state that SpectreRF rejects. Net: use laplace_nd unless you specifically need exposed physical state.

methods.name: Event/threshold-rich behavioral models (transition/slew/cross/last_crossing, @timer, local-state) for regulator digital wrappers (soft-start, current-limit, UVLO, PWM/PFM)
methods.what: Many regulator behaviors are inherently event-driven: soft-start ramps, current-limit foldback, UVLO comparators, enable sequencing, mode switching (PWM/PFM), and switching-converter control loops. The natural Verilog-A/AMS coding uses cross()/above() event detection, transition()/slew() for clean edges, @timer for periodic sampling, and integer/real local variables that remember state between events. Kundert's track-and-hold, divider and DFF examples are the canonical patterns.
methods.where_used: PMIC system models, switching-regulator/DC-DC behavioral models, supervisory/sequencing logic, and the digital-control portions of mixed-signal LDOs (digital LDOs). Heavily used in Verilog-AMS and VHDL-AMS PMIC simulation and in real-number-model (RNM) flows.
strengths: Naturally expresses sequencing/protection behavior that is awkward in pure controlled-source form
transition()/slew() give clean, timestep-controlled edges for transient and digital co-sim
Fast for system-level transient and functional verification of start-up/fault behavior
weaknesses: This is the family that BREAKS PSS/HB. Kundert: delay and z-domain transfer functions create hidden state and are OUTLAWED — Spectre aborts before RF analysis. transition/slew/cross/last_crossing also create hidden state (briefly), and random/dist_* both create hidden state and a non-periodic process unusable in PSS/QPSS
Local state variables (value used before set, or set only inside an event block) are detected and cause SpectreRF to terminate

Even when made hidden-state-free (idt-based DFF), an abstract sample/transition model can BLOCK small-signal propagation — the transition output is fixed by the large-signal solution, so a supply sideband does NOT propagate (Kundert's small-signal-propagation caveat). Fatal for spur/sideband fidelity

Workarounds (ignore_state / instrument_module attributes) only suppress the error message; they do NOT fix the underlying convergence/propagation problem and are only safe for measurement/stimulus blocks
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f)/PSRR(f): generally NOT modeled in this family (it is for digital/sequencing behavior); if a regulator core is wrapped this way, the small-signal supply path is often broken. Noise PSD: random/dist_* are unusable in PSS/QPSS. Discrete spurs: a switching-converter event model CAN generate deterministic tones in transient (switching ripple), but if it relies on hidden state it cannot be carried into PSS/HB, and if it uses transition() the sideband won't propagate in the small-signal phase. PSS/HB: WORST family for RF — must keep co-periodic with sources, avoid all hidden-state functions, and ensure a continuous signal path each period; otherwise non-convergent or silently wrong sidebands. For spur fidelity near a carrier, keep the supply path purely Laplace/ddt and quarantine event logic outside the RF small-signal path.

methods.name: Dedicated frequency-dependent PSRR injection technique for LDO behavioral models (auxiliary-network / supply-sensing transfer-function approach)
methods.what: A focused refinement (e.g., the MDPI Mathematics 2022 automotive-LDO PSRR work; op-amp PSRR macromodeling literature) that treats PSRR(f) as a first-class, accurately-fitted transfer function rather than a single DC number. The supply node is sensed, passed through a fitted multi-pole/zero network (Laplace operator or auxiliary RLC schematic with G/R/L/E ladders), and summed into the output. PSRR+ and PSRR- are handled separately. The 2022 automotive paper reports an optimized PSRR model accurate below ~500 kHz, motivated by the fact that legacy vendor LDO models had only crude/DC PSRR.
methods.where_used: Automotive/industrial LDO behavioral models where supply-ripple rejection across the converter switching band is safety/EMC-relevant; op-amp vendor macromodels (CMRR/PSRR sub-ladders). Implemented in both SPICE controlled-source form and Verilog-A laplace form.
strengths: Directly targets the hardest-to-model and most spur-relevant quantity — frequency-dependent supply rejection
Separate PSRR+/PSRR- and proper pole/zero fit give correct sideband magnitude across the supply-ripple band
Drop-in for AC, transient and (if Laplace/ddt-based) PSS/HB; relays a supply tone to the output as a phase-correct sideband
Matches the user's project architecture (signed-section SK-fit PSRR block, non-minimum-phase fix)
weaknesses: Stated validity is band-limited (the automotive model: below ~500 kHz) — near an RF carrier the PSRR fit may need to extend much higher in frequency and capture non-minimum-phase behavior
Still linear: it relays supply spurs but does not synthesize new ones from regulator nonlinearity
Quality is entirely fit-dependent: wrong phase or a missing non-minimum-phase zero gives wrong sideband phase/magnitude even if |PSRR| looks right
Requires careful extraction of PSRR from the real LDO (AC + possibly large-signal) to fit
methods.supply_handling: This technique IS the supply-handling method: PSRR(f) is the deliverable. Zout(f) is typically modeled by a companion network. Noise: add PSD sources. Discrete spurs: best-in-class for RELAYING a deterministic supply tone into a correct output sideband (magnitude+phase), which is exactly the LDO-as-supply-element-near-carrier use case; it does not itself create tones. PSS/HB: convergent and correct provided it is realized with laplace_nd or ddt/idt (supported state) and not with event/hidden-state constructs.

methods.name: Verilog-A/EDD models engineered specifically for Harmonic Balance (Brinson/Qucs RF device modeling discipline)
methods.what: A modeling discipline (Brinson & Kuznetsov, Qucs equation-defined-device + Verilog-A RF models for HB) for writing behavioral models that converge well in harmonic balance specifically. Core rules: keep as much of the model LINEAR as possible and confine nonlinearity to small, well-behaved equation-defined/Verilog-A blocks; ensure all branch constitutive relations are continuous and infinitely differentiable; reserve nonlinear sections so HB partitioning does not fail; avoid constructs that HB cannot Fourier-transform (hidden state, hard clamps, lookup discontinuities).
methods.where_used: Qucs/QucsStudio, Keysight ADS, and general HB-based RF flows; the methodology generalizes to any HB engine (Spectre HB, ADS HB). Directly relevant to modeling an LDO as a supply element inside an HB RF sim.
strengths: Explicit recipe for HB-convergent behavioral models: linear-dominant + small smooth nonlinear cores + C-infinity constitutive relations
Reduces HB 'partition failure' by keeping nonlinear partitions small
Compatible with the Laplace/ddt linear-supply-path approach the user needs

Aligns with Coram et al. 'Best Practices for Compact Modeling in Verilog-A': continuity and differentiability are MANDATORY for Newton/HB convergence; avoid hidden state; use $limexp instead of exp; smooth all transitions
weaknesses: A discipline/guideline rather than a turnkey LDO model — you still write the model
Strict smoothness can force approximation of genuinely sharp behavior (current limit, dropout knee) with smooth functions, trading a little accuracy for convergence
Not all behavioral authors follow it, so many existing vendor/IP models are NOT HB-clean
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f)/PSRR(f): unchanged in form (Laplace/ddt) but the discipline ensures they remain HB-convergent. Noise PSD: keep noise as small-signal PSD (white_noise/flicker_noise), excluded from the HB large-signal balance; use pnoise/HBnoise for sideband noise. Discrete spurs: if a deterministic tone must be self-generated, implement it as a small, smooth, co-periodic nonlinear contribution so HB can Fourier-resolve it; never via random/event/hidden-state. PSS/HB: this is the family whose entire purpose is robust PSS/HB convergence — the right lens for fast spur-fidelity LDO modeling.

findings.relevance_to_spur_pss: Highly relevant — this family is the correct foundation for the user's goal, and the survey resolves the core design questions. (1) For an LDO acting as a SUPPLY element whose only job is to relay/shape a supply tone into a sideband near an RF carrier, the spur band is linear (consistent with the user's own MEMORY finding that harmonics are <-62 dBc), so a LINEAR multiport behavioral model defined by Zout(f) and PSRR(f) is sufficient and is exactly what the controlled-source and Laplace families provide. (2) The single most important convergence fact, from Kundert's 'Hidden State in SpectreRF': SpectreRF/PSS supports ONLY the state held by ddt, idt, idtmod, and the Laplace filter functions (laplace_nd etc.). Therefore the supply path MUST be realized with laplace_nd or ddt/idt — NOT with delay, z-transform, transition, slew, cross, last_crossing, or random/dist_* (all create hidden state; some are outlawed outright and abort the run, others break or block small-signal propagation so the sideband silently vanishes). This directly tells the user how to keep the model PSS/HB-convergent and fast. (3) laplace_nd is the recommended realization for phase-correct PSRR(f)/Zout(f): it is the best-conditioned (Beckett: the equivalent hand-rolled high-order ddt ODE can fail at minimum timestep) and it is HB/PSS-legal. Non-minimum-phase fits matter (matches the user's V4 PSRR finding) because sideband PHASE depends on it. (4) For deterministic self-generated spurs (the user's spur block), pure linear models only RELAY supply tones; any self-generated tone must be a small, smooth, co-periodic nonlinear contribution per the Brinson/Coram HB discipline (continuous, C-infinity, no hidden state), or injected as a deterministic source — never via random()/event logic which is non-periodic and unusable in PSS/QPSS. (5) Noise should stay as PSD (white_noise/flicker_noise, or the Norton-at-vout loop-shaped form the user adopted) feeding pnoise/HBnoise, kept OUT of the large-signal HB balance. Net: build the LDO as a linear Laplace (laplace_nd) two-port in Zout(f)+PSRR(f), add smooth co-periodic deterministic spur sources, keep noise as PSD — this maximizes spur fidelity AND PSS/HB convergence/speed.
sources: Kenneth S. Kundert, 'Hidden State in SpectreRF', The Designer's Guide Community, v2 (2019/2024) — https://designers-guide.org/analysis/hidden-state.pdf (full text read: ddt/idt/idtmod/Laplace are the only SpectreRF-supported state; delay/z-transform outlawed; transition/slew/cross/last_crossing and random/dist_* cause hidden state; small-signal propagation caveat)

Geoffrey J. Coram et al., 'Best Practices for Compact Modeling in Verilog-A', IEEE — https://www.researchgate.net/publication/281227678_Best_Practices_for_Compact_Modeling_in_Verilog-A (continuity/differentiability mandatory; avoid hidden state; $limexp; convergence written into the model)

Cadence Community forum, 'Convergence difficulties in Verilog A while using equivalent differential equation instead of laplace_nd function' (Andrew Beckett, Cadence) — https://community.cadence.com/cadence_technology_forums/f/custom-ic-design/37413/ (laplace_nd converges where equivalent 3rd-order ddt ODE fails at min timestep; scale derivatives, custom natures/disciplines)

Cadence Community forum, 'How to avoid Hidden State in VerilogA model for SpectreRF' (Andrew Beckett) — https://community.cadence.com/cadence_technology_forums/f/rf-design/26712/ (keep every variable assigned every iteration; cross() + assignment pattern; idt-based state)

Nazar Shtybel (Renesas), 'Designing an Accurate SPICE Macromodel for Ultra-Low Power, Rail-to-Rail CMOS Op Amps', White Paper R19WP0020EE0101 (2017/2025) — https://www.renesas.com/en/document/whp/designing-accurate-spice-macromodel-ultra-low-power-rail-rail-cmos-op-amps (full text read: modern controlled-source macromodel; PSRR+/PSRR- as G-R-L-E ladders; noise = white R + 1/f diode; output-Z = G/R/L; references Boyle 1974 and Alexander/Bowers)
Boyle, Cohn, Pederson, Solomon, 'Macromodeling of Integrated Circuit Operational Amplifiers', IEEE JSSC vol. SC-9 no. 6, Dec 1974 (foundational controlled-source op-amp macromodel)

M. Brinson & V. Kuznetsov, 'Qucs equation-defined and Verilog-A RF device models for harmonic balance circuit simulation' — https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/161866839.pdf and https://repository.londonmet.ac.uk/3928/ (keep model linear-dominant, confine smooth nonlinearity, reduce HB partition failure)

'Optimized Power Supply Rejection Ratio Modeling Technique for Simulation of Automotive Low-Dropout Linear Voltage Regulators', MDPI Mathematics 10(7):1150, 2022 — https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7390/10/7/1150 (frequency-dependent PSRR behavioral model accurate below ~500 kHz; legacy LDO models had crude/DC PSRR)

'SPICE It Up: Understanding and Using Op-Amp Macromodels', Electronic Design — https://www.electronicdesign.com/technologies/analog/article/21806271/ (Boyle three-stage VCVS/VCCS structure and limitations)

findings.category: Black-box system identification & rational/passive macromodeling (signal-/power-integrity world): fitting measured/simulated Zout(f), PSRR(f), Y/Z/S data into pole-residue state-space models and synthesizing passive SPICE/Verilog-A equivalent circuits

methods.name: Vector Fitting (VF) — Gustavsen-Semlyen, incl. Relaxed VF (RVF) and Orthonormal VF
methods.what: The workhorse of the family. VF fits tabulated complex frequency-response data H(jw) (a scalar, or each entry of a Y/Z/S matrix, or PSRR(jw)) to a rational pole-residue model H(s) = d + s*e + sum_k c_k/(s - p_k). It is an iterative reformulation of the Sanathanan-Koerner (SK) iteration cast in a partial-fraction (barycentric-like) basis: starting from an initial pole set, it solves a linear least-squares problem for residues plus the residues of a 'scaling' sigma(s); the zeros of sigma become the relocated poles for the next iteration. Real poles give first-order sections, complex-conjugate pairs give second-order sections, and the model is forced to have stable (LHP) poles by flipping any RHP poles. Relaxed VF (Gustavsen 2006) removes the rigid high-frequency asymptotic =1 constraint on sigma, dramatically improving pole relocation and convergence especially on noisy data; Orthonormal VF (Deschrijver/Dhaene) replaces partial fractions with orthonormal rational basis functions for better conditioning at high order. Matrix/fast VF shares a common pole set across all matrix entries for a compact MIMO macromodel.
methods.where_used: Reference implementation vectfit3.m (SINTEF, Gustavsen). Python: scikit-rf skrf.vectorFitting (vector_fit, auto_fit, passivity_test, passivity_enforce, write_spice_subcircuit_s). Commercial: IdEM / IdemWorks (Grivet-Talocia, now part of Dassault SIMULIA SI/PI flow), Keysight ADS broadband SPICE / 'Fit' models, Cadence Sigrity/PowerSI, Simberian Simbeor. Dominant in power-systems EMTP, high-speed interconnect/package SI, and PDN/PI impedance modeling.
strengths: Robust, mature, well-documented; de-facto industry standard for turning measured/simulated Z(f)/Y(f)/S(f) into a state-space + SPICE model.
Directly applicable to an LDO's measured/simulated Zout(f) (as a 1-port Z or Y) and PSRR(f) (as a 2-port transfer / controlled-source path).
Guarantees stable poles; pairs naturally with passivity enforcement to give SPICE-convergent, energy-bounded models.
Output is exactly a sum of RLC sections + controlled sources, which simulators (incl. PSS/HB) handle as ideal linear elements — fast and analytically Jacobian-friendly.
weaknesses: Pure LS fit weights by |data| and frequency sampling; small-but-important features (a sharp PSRR notch, a high-Q Zout resonance) need adequate sample density and sometimes inverse-magnitude weighting or they get under-fit.
Passivity enforcement and the minimum-phase assumption are in tension with genuinely non-minimum-phase LDO PSRR (RHP zeros / sign reversals / loop delay) — see supply_handling.
Order selection is manual-ish (n_poles_real/n_poles_cmplx); too few poles miss resonances, too many create spurious near-cancelling pole/zero pairs that hurt HB/PSS Jacobian conditioning.
Noisy data degrades the fit (relaxed VF helps but VF still interpolates noise to some degree).
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): excellent — a 1-port Z(s) or Y(s) pole-residue model captures the LDO output resonance (the load-step ring) with correct magnitude AND phase, which is exactly what governs transient and what a passive RLC realization reproduces. PSRR(f): good for magnitude/phase but the catch is non-minimum-phase content (the model memory notes a non-min-phase V4 PSRR with sign reversals): standard passive VF assumes a minimum-phase/passive response; a true RHP zero or net loop delay must be modeled explicitly (signed sections / cascaded delay+rational), otherwise VF either mis-fits the phase or the passivity step distorts it. Noise PSD: VF is for transfer/immittance functions, not stochastic sources — but it is the right tool to fit the SHAPING filter |Zout(jw)| or the loop-shape so a Norton noise source Sin = Sv*|Yout|^2 can be injected at vout (matches the project's shaped-Norton approach). Discrete spurs: VF cannot represent deterministic tones — spurs are not part of a rational immittance and must be added as separate periodic sources; VF only provides the linear transfer that the spur propagates through. PSS/HB: ideal citizen — once realized as RLC + linear controlled sources the macromodel is exactly linear, so it contributes a constant (frequency-evaluated) admittance stamp into HB and a linear block into shooting/PSS with no convergence penalty and analytic derivatives; this is the single biggest argument for VF in this application.

methods.name: Sanathanan-Koerner (SK) iteration / Levy linearized rational LS
methods.what: The classical ancestor (Levy 1959 linearized; Sanathanan-Koerner 1963 iteratively reweighted). Writes H(jw) ~ N(jw)/D(jw), multiplies through by D to linearize (Levy), then iteratively reweights each LS solve by 1/|D_prev| so the solution converges to the true weighted nonlinear LS fit rather than the magnitude-biased Levy fit. VF is essentially SK in a partial-fraction basis with explicit pole flipping for stability; modern 'stabilized SK' variants (e.g. Hochman) add orthogonal polynomial bases to fix the notorious ill-conditioning of the monomial Vandermonde system at wide bandwidth/high order.
methods.where_used: Foundational in control/system-ID textbooks and MATLAB-style transfer-function fitting; underlies invfreqs/invfreqz-type routines and the theory behind VF. Used directly when a numerator/denominator polynomial form (rather than pole-residue) is wanted — e.g. fitting a compact PSRR(s) for a Verilog-A Laplace block.
strengths: Conceptually simple; gives a closed-form rational TF (num/den) directly usable in Verilog-A laplace_nd / s-domain blocks.
Natural for low-order LDO loop transfer functions where you actually want zeros (incl. RHP zeros) explicit.
Stabilized (orthogonal-basis) versions are competitive with VF in accuracy.
weaknesses: Naive monomial implementation is severely ill-conditioned over wide frequency spans (exactly the LDO mHz-to-100MHz span), causing garbage high-order fits — this is precisely why VF replaced it for SI work.
No built-in stability guarantee (poles can land in RHP); must post-process.
Iteration can stall or oscillate on noisy/resonant data.
methods.supply_handling: Zout/PSRR: can fit both as N(s)/D(s); the advantage over VF is that RHP zeros in a non-minimum-phase PSRR are represented naturally in the numerator, so it is arguably the cleaner route for the project's signed/non-min-phase PSRR block. Noise PSD / spurs: same as VF — SK fits transfer functions only, not sources or tones. PSS/HB: if realized as a linear laplace block or RLC it is as convergence-friendly as VF; but if left as a high-order ill-conditioned polynomial, HB Jacobian stamps can be poorly scaled. Practical recommendation: use SK/stabilized-SK when you want explicit RHP zeros in PSRR(s), use VF when you want a guaranteed-passive RLC Zout.

methods.name: Loewner framework (LF) — Mayo-Antoulas, tangential interpolation
methods.what: A direct, essentially non-iterative data-driven realization method. From frequency samples it partitions the data into 'left' and 'right' sets and builds the Loewner matrix L and shifted-Loewner matrix Ls; for MIMO it uses tangential (left/right direction-vector) interpolation. An SVD of [L, Ls] (and the pencil) reveals the true minimal McMillan degree: the numerical rank tells you the model order, and truncating the SVD gives a reduced descriptor/state-space realization (E,A,B,C) that interpolates the data. No initial pole guess, no SK-style iteration.
methods.where_used: Strong in the model-order-reduction / systems-and-control community (Antoulas, Beattie, Gugercin) and increasingly in SI/microwave macromodeling and EM data reduction. Appears in research macromodeling flows and some MOR toolboxes; used for very-high-port-count S-parameter reduction.
strengths: Non-iterative and pole-guess-free — avoids VF's convergence sensitivity entirely.
Automatically exposes the minimal order via the singular-value drop, giving a principled order-selection (useful to find the smallest LDO Zout/PSRR model that still rings correctly).
Naturally MIMO/multiport via tangential interpolation (good if Zout, PSRR, and a load-port are modeled as one multiport).
weaknesses: Raw LF interpolates noise (it forces exact interpolation), so on measured/noisy LDO data it overfits unless regularized (least-squares Loewner) — a documented weakness.

Produces a descriptor (E,A,B,C) realization that is not inherently passive or even guaranteed stable; needs the same passivity/stability post-processing as VF, and SPICE synthesis from a descriptor form is less standardized than from VF pole-residue.
Less turnkey tooling than VF for the SPICE-export step; more of a research/MOR tool.
methods.supply_handling: Zout/PSRR: fits both accurately and gives the minimal order, attractive when you want the smallest convergent macromodel. Non-minimum-phase: LF makes no minimum-phase assumption, so RHP zeros are captured as naturally as any other data — a genuine advantage over passive VF for the V4-style PSRR. Noise PSD: again a transfer-function tool, used for the shaping filter, not the stochastic source. Discrete spurs: not representable. PSS/HB: once converted to state-space/RLC it is linear and HB/PSS-friendly like VF; but the lack of built-in passivity means an un-enforced descriptor model can inject negative-resistance behavior that breaks HB/shooting convergence — passivity must be enforced before trusting it in PSS.

methods.name: AAA algorithm (adaptive Antoulas-Anderson) — barycentric rational approximation
methods.what: Modern (Nakatsukasa-Sete-Trefethen, SIAM 2018) rational approximation in barycentric form with greedy adaptive selection of support/interpolation points plus a linearized LS solve for barycentric weights. Yields poles/residues (via a generalized eigenvalue problem on the barycentric form) with near-best accuracy, no user-supplied pole count, and strong numerical stability. A natural competitor/complement to VF and LF for the fitting step.
methods.where_used: Originated in numerical-analysis (Chebfun/Oxford) but rapidly adopted for frequency-response and nonlinear-eigenvalue/rational fitting; used in research SI/EM contexts and as a robust front-end that can seed VF or feed a realization. set-valued/MIMO and weighted variants exist.
strengths: Extremely robust and parameter-free (no initial poles, automatic order via greedy stopping) — avoids both SK ill-conditioning and VF pole-init sensitivity.
Barycentric form is well-conditioned even over the wide LDO frequency span; gives clean poles/residues for subsequent RLC synthesis.
Handles sharp features (PSRR notches, Zout resonances) gracefully because support points cluster adaptively where the response varies fast.
weaknesses: Does not enforce stability or passivity natively — extracted poles can be RHP and must be filtered/flipped, and passivity enforced separately.
Less established than VF in production SI EDA SPICE-export flows; you typically use AAA to FIT then hand off to a VF/state-space realization+passivity step.
Weighting for relative (dB) accuracy on PSRR needs care.
methods.supply_handling: Zout/PSRR: high-accuracy magnitude+phase fit with automatic order; arguably best-in-class for capturing a high-Q Zout resonance with few poles. Non-minimum-phase: no min-phase assumption, RHP zeros captured directly. Noise PSD: shaping-filter fitting only, like the others. Spurs: not representable. PSS/HB: only as convergence-friendly as the realization you build from its poles/residues — after stability filtering and RLC/state-space synthesis it is a linear block identical in HB behavior to a VF model. Practical role here: a robust fitter to seed/replace VF, especially for tricky PSRR shapes.

methods.name: State-space realization + passivity enforcement (Hamiltonian / residue-perturbation)
methods.what: The 'make it physical and convergent' stage applied after any fitter (VF/LF/AAA/SK). The pole-residue model is realized as a state-space (A,B,C,D[,E]) system. Passivity is then (1) verified — the standard fast test forms a Hamiltonian (or half-size singularity-test) matrix whose purely-imaginary eigenvalues mark frequency bands where the model violates passivity (Re{Y}<0 or |S|>1); and (2) enforced — by perturbing residues / Hamiltonian eigenvalues (Grivet-Talocia 2004 Hamiltonian perturbation; Gustavsen fast residue-perturbation; Semlyen-Gustavsen half-size test) to push all eigenvalues off the imaginary axis with minimal change to the fit. DC-preserving and accuracy-constrained variants exist.
methods.where_used: Built into IdEM/IdemWorks, scikit-rf (passivity_test/passivity_enforce), Keysight/Cadence broadband-model generators, EMTP. The canonical reference is Grivet-Talocia & Gustavsen, 'Passive Macromodeling: Theory and Applications' (Wiley 2016).
strengths: Guarantees the macromodel cannot generate energy — the key to STABLE time-domain AND PSS/HB/shooting simulation; a non-passive Zout model is the classic cause of HB/transient divergence.
Fast modern tests (half-size, Hamiltonian) make verification cheap even at high order/port count.
Enforcement preserves accuracy within a user tolerance and can preserve DC exactly (important for an LDO's regulated DC operating point).
weaknesses: Passivity is the WRONG constraint for an active element: an LDO with PSRR < 0 dB (gain from supply) or with regions where it appears to deliver energy is legitimately non-passive, so blindly enforcing passivity on the PSRR path can corrupt the model. Passivity should be applied to the genuinely passive Zout port, NOT to active gain/PSRR paths.
Enforcement is iterative and can fail to converge or over-perturb when violations are large.
Non-minimum-phase responses are not made causal/physical by passivity enforcement — that needs delay extraction (next method).
methods.supply_handling: Zout: enforce passivity here — a passive Norton/RLC Zout is exactly right and is what keeps load-step transients and HB stable. PSRR: do NOT force passivity (it is an active transfer path); use stability-only constraints and explicit RHP-zero/sign handling. Noise PSD: passive realization of the shaping immittance ensures the injected Norton noise sees a physical, stable filter. Spurs: orthogonal to this step. PSS/HB convergence: THIS is the lever — passivity + stability of the linear macromodel is the dominant factor in whether SpectreRF PSS/HB and Spectre HB converge near the carrier; a passive, low-order, well-scaled state-space/RLC block stamps cleanly and keeps the Newton/shooting Jacobian well-conditioned.

methods.name: SPICE/Verilog-A equivalent-circuit synthesis from pole-residue (RLC + controlled-source realization)
methods.what: Turns the rational model into a netlist the simulator treats as ideal linear elements. Each real pole c/(s-p) becomes a first-order section (parallel RC for impedance form, series RL for admittance form: R, C=1/c, etc.); each complex-conjugate pole pair becomes a second-order RLC section, often with a voltage/current-controlled source (gyrator or VCCS) to realize the required (possibly negative) residue sign and to map a residue/pole pair to physical R,L,C values; the constant d and proportional s*e terms become a resistor and an inductor (impedance) or resistor and capacitor (admittance). Gustavsen 'SPICE equivalent circuits of frequency-domain responses' (IEEE TEMC 2002) and the Wiley book give the canonical branch formulas; scikit-rf write_spice_subcircuit_s emits a controlled-source state-space netlist directly. For Verilog-A, the same model is emitted as a laplace_nd / pole-residue analog block or as the shaped-Norton + PSRR-path structure used in the project.
methods.where_used: IdEM SPICE-link, scikit-rf, Keysight ADS, Cadence Sigrity/Spectre broadband models; the project itself emits SPICE subckt + Verilog-A from fitted Zout/PSRR.
strengths: Produces a portable, simulator-agnostic block (works in ngspice, Spectre, HSPICE, ADS) — matches the project's ngspice-subprocess + Verilog-A emission.
RLC + linear controlled sources are exactly the elements PSS/HB/shooting handle most efficiently (constant stamps, analytic Jacobians) — best possible convergence behavior.
Human-inspectable: poles/resonances map to recognizable LC sections (the Zout resonance is literally an LC tank).
weaknesses: Negative R/L/C from sign-indefinite residues require controlled sources; some simulators dislike negative elements and convergence/initialization can suffer if not realized via VCCS/gyrator.
Constant + proportional (d, s*e) terms and any net delay are awkward to realize purely with R/L/C (the s*e term is a pure inductor/capacitor that can dominate at band edges and add stiffness).

Verilog-A laplace blocks are convenient but some are evaluated differently (or with hidden interpolation) in HB vs transient, occasionally hurting spur fidelity — a hand-built RLC/controlled-source netlist is safer for PSS/HB.
methods.supply_handling: Zout: realize as a passive Norton (current source in parallel with synthesized Y(s) RLC) — directly supports injecting the shaped noise current and the deterministic spur currents at vout. PSRR: realize as a VCCS/E-source path from the supply node to vout with the fitted transfer (allowing the active, non-passive gain and RHP-zero sign). Noise PSD: the synthesized passive Zout doubles as the noise-shaping network; white+Lorentzian Norton sources injected across it reproduce the loop-shaped output noise. Discrete spurs: implemented as independent periodic current/voltage sources at vout at the measured tone frequencies/phases — this is THE place spurs live, and PSS/HB then folds them to f_RF +/- m*f_spur sidebands. PSS/HB: a clean RLC+controlled-source subckt is the most convergence-robust representation; avoids the per-iteration cost and possible HB-mode artifacts of a behavioral laplace block.

methods.name: Delay-extraction / non-minimum-phase macromodeling (DEPACT, delayed VF)
methods.what: Handles responses whose phase is NOT minimum-phase — i.e. they contain pure transport delay or RHP zeros that a finite stable pole-residue model cannot represent without huge order. The method factors H(s) = e^{-s*tau} * H_rational(s) (delay extracted by Brent-method optimization or causal Fourier continuation / Hilbert-transform causality check), or builds a cascade of delay + rational sections (DEPACT). Delayed/iterative VF optimizes tau jointly with poles/residues. RHP zeros are then either left in the numerator (non-passive but stable) or approximated by an all-pass/delay factor.
methods.where_used: High-speed interconnect / long-transmission-line macromodeling (Achar, Nakhla, Grivet-Talocia, Charest); relevant whenever measured phase shows net group delay beyond what poles explain.
strengths: Correctly represents net loop delay / RHP-zero phase lag with low rational order — directly relevant to a fast LDO loop whose PSRR phase rolls past minimum-phase expectation.
Keeps the rational part low-order and well-conditioned, which is good for HB/PSS Jacobian quality.
Causality-checked (Hilbert/Kramers-Kronig) so the model does not respond before excitation.
weaknesses: A pure delay e^{-s*tau} is itself hard for HB/PSS: in HB it is an exact per-harmonic phase rotation (fine), but in shooting/transient it needs a delay line / Pade approximation that can re-introduce poles and hurt convergence.
Joint delay+pole optimization is more fragile than plain VF.
Overkill for an LDO if the non-min-phase content is just one RHP zero — a signed second-order section (as the project already does) is simpler.
methods.supply_handling: Zout: usually minimum-phase, so delay extraction rarely needed. PSRR: THE relevant tool when the project's non-min-phase V4 PSRR has net group delay rather than a single RHP zero — extract tau, fit the rest rationally, and in HB the delay is an exact phase term per harmonic (good for spur-phase fidelity around the carrier). Noise PSD: not applicable (sources, not transfer). Discrete spurs: spur PHASE relative to carrier depends on the PSRR/Zout phase the spur sees; getting the non-min-phase phase right (via delay extraction) is what makes the predicted sideband phase/asymmetry correct. PSS/HB: HB tolerates pure delay well (exact in frequency domain), which actually favors HB over shooting/transient for non-min-phase LDO supply modeling near an RF carrier; in shooting-PSS use a low-order Pade of the delay to stay convergent.

findings.relevance_to_spur_pss: Very high relevance for the LINEAR transfer/immittance part of the problem, low relevance for the stochastic-source and tone parts. This family is exactly the right way to convert simulated/measured LDO Zout(f) and PSRR(f) into compact, stable, passive (for Zout) pole-residue state-space models and then into RLC + controlled-source SPICE/Verilog-A blocks. That representation is the single most convergence-friendly thing you can hand to Cadence SpectreRF PSS/Spectre HB/shooting: linear RLC + linear controlled sources produce constant frequency-evaluated stamps with analytic Jacobians, no per-iteration nonlinear re-evaluation, and (if passivity is enforced on the genuinely passive Zout port) a guaranteed energy-bounded block that will not cause HB/PSS divergence. The project's own findings line up with this: the spur band is linear (harmonics <-62 dBc), the transient is governed by Zout(s), and noise is a loop-shaped Norton across Zout — all of which are precisely what rational macromodeling captures. Critical caveats specific to spur/PSS fidelity: (1) Passivity enforcement must NOT be applied to the active PSRR/gain path (PSRR<0 dB is legitimately non-passive); enforce passivity only on Zout, and use stability-only + explicit RHP-zero/delay handling for PSRR. (2) Non-minimum-phase PSRR (RHP zeros / net loop delay, the project's V4 problem) is the main failure mode of vanilla passive VF — use signed second-order sections, stabilized-SK with explicit numerator zeros, the delay-extraction variant, or AAA/Loewner (which make no min-phase assumption). (3) This family CANNOT model the two things spur fidelity ultimately needs as sources: the discrete spur TONES and the noise PSD. Those must be added as deterministic periodic current/voltage sources and as shaped Norton noise sources injected at vout; rational macromodeling supplies only the linear network through which they propagate. In PSS/HB the linear macromodel then correctly folds an injected f_spur and the noise PSD into f_RF +/- m*f_spur sidebands (cyclostationary noise folding), so getting Zout/PSRR magnitude AND phase right via VF/AAA/Loewner is what makes the sideband amplitudes and phase/asymmetry correct. Net: adopt this family for the Zout/PSRR blocks (VF or AAA fit -> stability filter -> passivity enforce on Zout only -> RLC/controlled-source synthesis), and keep spurs/noise as separate injected sources.
