findings.category: Nonlinear frequency-domain behavioral models for harmonic balance / PSS (X-parameters / PHD, hot S-parameters, large-signal scattering functions, envelope/memory extensions, and supply representation in HB/PSS/shooting RF flows)

methods.name: Poly-Harmonic Distortion (PHD) model / X-parameters (spectral linearization around a large-signal operating point)
methods.what: A black-box frequency-domain map that linearizes a device's harmonic spectrum around a large-signal periodic operating point set by one (or more) 'large' tones. The canonical single-large-tone form for each scattered spectral component is b_pk = X_F_pk(|A11|) * P^k + sum over (q,l) of [ X_S_pk,ql(|A11|) * P^(k-l) * a_ql  +  X_T_pk,ql(|A11|) * P^(k+l) * conj(a_ql) ], where P = exp(j*phase(A11)) is the phase reference (LSOP phase normalization). X_F is the large-signal harmonic response, X_S/X_T are the sensitivities of harmonic k to small perturbations a at harmonic l. The conjugate (X_T) term is mandatory because the dependence on small signals is 'non-analytic linear' (depends on both a and conj(a)) under large-signal drive — this is the same fact behind 'hot S22 needs 6 not 4 parameters'. In the small-signal limit X_T->0 and X-params reduce to S-params. Extracted from NVNA measurement or from HB sim of a transistor-level netlist; consumed directly by HB simulators (ADS X-parameter generator/component).
methods.where_used: Keysight/Agilent (David E. Root, Jan Verspecht, Jason Horn, Mihai Marcu) — productized in Keysight ADS and on PNA-X/NVNA instruments; Modelithics ships X-parameter device models for ADS. The de-facto industrial nonlinear behavioral standard for PA/transistor/MMIC blocks in HB design flows. Textbook: Root/Verspecht/Horn/Marcu, 'X-Parameters' (Cambridge, 2013).
strengths: Cascadable: X-params of individually characterized blocks compose to predict a chain's nonlinear transfer, all inside HB
Correctly predicts harmonic amplitudes/phases AND small-tone sidebands/mixing onto each harmonic (X_S/X_T), so it captures spur generation around a driven LSOP
Native HB citizen — frequency-domain table that the simulator evaluates per spectral line; very fast vs transistor-level HB
Exact reduction to S-parameters / hot-S in appropriate limits, so it degrades gracefully toward linear regimes
weaknesses: Standard form is referenced to a SINGLE large tone (the carrier); validity of the spectral linearization degrades when a second perturbation grows large (it is a first-order/Jacobian map in the small a, not a full multi-large-tone model)
Load dependence: classic X-params assume a near-50ohm LSOP; load-dependent X-params require load-tuner NVNA acquisition or embedding and add cost/dimensionality
Table-based interpolation of X_F/X_S/X_T over (power, freq, bias, load) can create derivative/smoothness issues that hurt HB convergence if not C1/C2-continuous
Captures device/PA nonlinear scattering, not specifically a power-rail-as-input element; using it for an LDO supply port means treating the supply as a bias dimension, which inflates the model
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): only as the harmonic-l self term X_S_2k,2k (output reflection at each harmonic) around the LSOP — i.e. a 'hot' output admittance, NOT a clean wideband Zout(f). PSRR(f)/supply modulation: only if bias/supply is added as an explicit X-parameter dimension (augmented/bias-dependent X-params); standard X-params do not model a supply input port at all. Noise PSD: not part of core X-params (noise is handled by a separate cyclostationary/Pnoise mechanism). Discrete spurs: predicted as small-signal sidebands at fc +/- fd via X_S (lower-sideband-from-a) and X_T (upper-sideband-from-conj-a) — this is exactly the mechanism for supply-ripple-induced sidebands IF the ripple is injected as a small a-wave. Under HB/PSS: it IS the frequency-domain HB primitive, so 'robustness under HB' = good provided the underlying tables are smooth and the perturbation stays small.

methods.name: Hot S-parameters / large-signal small-signal (LSSS) scattering ('6 = 4 + 2')
methods.what: Small-signal S-parameters measured/defined around an active large-signal operating point (e.g. a PA driven by a power tone). Verspecht's key result is that a self-consistent description needs SIX real parameters per port pair, not four: b2 = S22*a2 + T22*conj(a2) (plus the S21 driven response), because the relationship between the small reflected wave b2 and small incident a2 is non-analytic — there is both an S term (acts on a2) and a T term (acts on conj(a2), generating the image/lower sideband). Probe tone at fc+fd produces measurable responses at fc, fc+fd AND fc-fd; the fc-fd (conjugate/image) response is what forces the T term. This is the conceptual seed of the PHD/X-parameter X_S/X_T split (Verspecht patent US7038468B2, assignee Keysight).
methods.where_used: Jan Verspecht (VUB PhD, multi-frequency Describing Functions) and Marc Vanden Bossche/NMDG, later Agilent/Keysight; ARFTG community. Used as the measurement-theoretic foundation under X-parameters; 'hot S22' is a standard PA stability/output-match characterization on PNA-X.
strengths: Rigorous, measurement-validated treatment of how a SMALL perturbation (e.g. supply ripple, interferer) scatters around a driven carrier — directly the spur/sideband problem
Makes explicit that upper and lower sidebands are coupled (S vs T / conjugate term) — essential for correct sideband phase and asymmetry
Reduces to ordinary S-parameters when the large signal -> 0; intuitive extension of linear two-port theory
weaknesses: A linearization: valid only while the perturbation is small relative to the carrier-set LSOP (true for low-level supply spurs, breaks for large ripple)
Defined per fixed LSOP (power, bias, load); a sweep of operating points is needed to cover a design space
Pure two-port RF framing — does not by itself give a wideband noise PSD or a low-frequency PSRR(f); needs augmentation
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): yes, as 'hot' output reflection (hot-S22) at the carrier and harmonics around the LSOP. PSRR(f): only if a supply/bias port is added as a third measured port (conversion S-params from supply-tone to RF sidebands) — this conversion-matrix view is the most rigorous way to express PSRR as a large-signal effect. Noise: not native. Discrete spurs: this is precisely the framework's strength — a discrete supply tone at fc+/-fd maps through S (and conjugate T) to coupled sidebands with correct relative phase. PSS/HB: the S/T conversion matrix is exactly what PAC/PXF (HB or shooting) compute as the periodic small-signal transfer; hot-S is the analytic interpretation of those simulator outputs.

methods.name: Augmented / bias- and load-dependent X-parameters and supply-as-input (envelope-tracking) behavioral models
methods.what: Extensions that add the DC/bias (drain/collector supply) and/or load reflection as explicit model dimensions so that the supply voltage becomes a large-signal input, not just a fixed bias. The supply can then be modulated (envelope tracking) and the model predicts how supply variation remaps gain/phase and generates sidebands/spectral regrowth. Includes simulation- and measurement-based X-parameter models for ET-PAs (supply applied at drain/collector) and load-dependent X-params acquired with an NVNA + load tuner.
methods.where_used: Keysight ADS ET-PA design flows; academic/industrial PA groups (Root, Verspecht, Horn et al.; envelope-tracking literature). Used where the supply IS the modulating input (ET-PAs, DPD verification).
strengths: Treats the supply rail as a first-class large-signal input — the correct framing when a regulator's output modulates an RF stage
Captures cross-modulation between supply (envelope) and RF carrier, i.e. supply-induced sidebands as a large-signal (not just small-signal) effect
Still lives inside HB/Circuit-Envelope flows; reuses the X-parameter consumer in ADS
weaknesses: Dimensionality explosion: tables over (Pin, freq, Vsupply, load) are costly to acquire and store, and interpolation smoothness becomes a real HB-convergence liability
Designed for the PA/transistor use case (supply at output of an active device), not for characterizing the regulator block itself
Long-term memory (electrothermal/trapping) still needs the separate memory extension; quasi-static augmented X-params can mispredict low-offset asymmetric sidebands
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): hot output impedance per (bias,power) point. PSRR(f): represented implicitly as the supply-port-to-RF-sideband conversion sensitivity at each LSOP — effectively a large-signal PSRR that varies with drive. Noise: not native. Discrete spurs: a discrete supply ripple tone is handled either as a small perturbation (X_S/X_T at the supply port) or, if large, by sampling the envelope axis — good fidelity for ET-style supply modulation. PSS/HB: integrates as an HB/Envelope component; convergence depends entirely on table smoothness across the added supply axis.

methods.name: X-parameters with long-term dynamic memory (short-term vs long-term / electrothermal & trapping)
methods.what: Adds memory to the otherwise quasi-static spectral map: short-term (modulation-bandwidth) memory and long-term memory from bias/supply networks, self-heating, and charge trapping. The instantaneous spectral response is made a function of slow state variables (temperature, trap occupancy, dynamic bias) so that asymmetric sidebands and bias-/thermal-dependent IM and spectral regrowth under modulated drive are captured (Verspecht et al., IMS 2009/2010; Root/Verspecht/Horn/Marcu book Ch. 6 'Memory').
methods.where_used: Keysight research + academia; relevant to wideband modulated PAs where supply/thermal dynamics produce memory. Implemented as memory-augmented X-parameter / dynamic-behavioral models in ADS Circuit Envelope.
strengths: Captures the slow supply/thermal dynamics that make sidebands ASYMMETRIC and frequency-offset dependent — directly relevant to low-offset supply spurs
Bridges to envelope/transient-envelope analysis where the carrier is fixed but the envelope (and supply) evolves
Physically motivated decomposition (short- vs long-term) maps onto LDO loop bandwidth vs thermal/bias timescales
weaknesses: More parameters and identification effort; risk of overfitting the memory kernels
Heavier to evaluate; can slow HB/Envelope and complicate convergence
Still aimed at active-device memory, not a turnkey regulator-supply model
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): hot, now with dynamic (memory) variation. PSRR(f): the most complete X-param-family route to a frequency-dependent, asymmetric supply-to-output conversion because memory lets the sideband response differ at +fd vs -fd. Noise: not native (still separate). Discrete spurs: best-in-family for asymmetric, low-offset supply spurs because long-term memory reproduces sideband asymmetry that quasi-static models miss. PSS/HB: typically run in Circuit Envelope (carrier in HB, envelope in time) rather than pure multitone HB; convergence is generally acceptable but evaluation cost is higher.

methods.name: PSS/shooting + PAC/PXF/Pnoise small-signal periodic analyses (the simulator-side counterpart to hot-S/X-params)
methods.what: Cadence SpectreRF-style flow: PSS (shooting-Newton in time, or HB in frequency) computes the large-signal periodic steady state set by the RF carrier; then PAC/PXF compute the periodic small-signal conversion matrix (how a small tone at any offset maps to every sideband fk +/- fm), and Pnoise computes cyclostationary noise folded to sidebands. Injecting a small supply-ripple tone at the rail and reading the resulting carrier sidebands IS the simulator-computed hot-S/X_S/X_T for the supply port. Ken Kundert's 'Simulation Methods for RF ICs' is the canonical reference; shooting is robust for stiff/strongly-switching circuits, HB for mildly-nonlinear/distortion problems.
methods.where_used: Cadence Spectre/SpectreRF (PSS/PAC/PXF/Pnoise/Pstb), Keysight ADS HB+Envelope, industry standard for RFIC. The user's own Cadence PSS/HB target flow.
strengths: Ground truth for spur fidelity: PAC/PXF give the exact periodic conversion gains incl. correct upper/lower sideband (conjugate) phase, and Pnoise gives folded noise PSD — the trio covers Zout, PSRR-as-conversion, noise, and discrete spurs in one consistent framework
Shooting-PSS handles strongly nonlinear/switching supplies where HB struggles; HB handles many-tone CW distortion where shooting is expensive
Directly accepts behavioral blocks (Verilog-A/AMS, data tables, S-param/rational models) as the supply element
weaknesses: Transistor-level cost is exactly what behavioral LDO models are meant to avoid; PSS+PXF over a fine offset sweep is slow at the system level

Shooting-Pnoise can MISS low-frequency supply-noise upconversion for large-time-constant supply nodes (documented SpectreRF caveat) — i.e. the very LDO/PSRR mechanism can be under-reported if the model lumps a slow supply pole
HB convergence is fragile with sharp/discontinuous behavioral nonlinearities; PSS convergence sensitive to tstab/steadyratio and stiff supply loops
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): PSS+PAC port impedance at carrier and sidebands. PSRR(f): inject supply tone, read carrier sidebands via PAC/PXF — the rigorous large-signal PSRR. Noise PSD: Pnoise gives cyclostationary, sideband-folded output noise (matches the project's loop-shaped Norton finding). Discrete spurs: directly produced by injecting deterministic supply tones into PSS/PAC; phase and asymmetry come out correctly. PSS/HB convergence: this is the reference flow — its caveats (large-time-constant supply nodes, behavioral discontinuities) are exactly the things a good LDO behavioral model must respect.

methods.name: Frequency-domain rational-fit (vector-fitting / pole-residue) behavioral macromodels of the regulator itself
methods.what: Instead of a device-scattering map, fit the LDO's measured/simulated frequency responses — output impedance Zout(f), PSRR(f), and reference/control-to-output transfer — to rational pole-residue functions (vector fitting), then synthesize an equivalent passive/active network (or Laplace/Verilog-A block) for the simulator. A dedicated USPTO patent (10,599,799) does exactly this for LDOs and charge pumps; vector fitting is the workhorse for guaranteed-passive, causal, low-order multiport macromodels. This is the family the user's project already lives in (Zout + PSRR + shaped-Norton noise + deterministic spur tones).
methods.where_used: Power-IC vendors and SI/PI macromodeling (vector fitting from Gustavsen/Semlyen lineage); LDO SPICE macromodels (Rohm, TI, ADI app notes); patent 10,599,799 (LDO/charge-pump frequency-domain fitting). Targets SPICE AC/transient primarily, but the synthesized rational/Laplace block is HB/PSS-consumable.
strengths: Models the SUPPLY ELEMENT directly and compactly: Zout(f), PSRR(f), control transfer as low-order rational functions — exactly the quantities that set spur transfer
Passivity/causality can be enforced (passive synthesis), which is what gives clean, convergent HB/PSS behavior
Multiport-capable (supply-in, ref-in, load-out) so PSRR and Zout share one consistent state-space; adds a Norton noise source and deterministic spur tones for the full picture
Cheap to evaluate in HB/PSS — a handful of poles vs a transistor netlist
weaknesses: Linear/LTI by construction: captures Zout/PSRR/noise/spur-transfer in the regime where the LDO behaves linearly (the project's own finding: linear to ~50uA, gm compression by ~1mA, slew at ~5mA) — large-signal load steps / current limit / dropout need added nonlinearity
Discrete output spurs are added as explicit deterministic sources, not generated by the physics — must be measured/characterized and re-injected with correct amplitude/phase per operating point
Rational fits can introduce non-minimum-phase / RHP or non-passive artifacts (the project hit a non-minimum-phase PSRR section) that must be policed before HB
Quality depends on the fidelity of the underlying frequency-domain data and on smooth interpolation across bias/load if the model is operating-point-scheduled
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): native and exact-by-fit (Norton/Thevenin rational). PSRR(f): native as a fitted supply-to-output transfer (signed/sectioned fits to get phase right). Noise PSD: add an output-referred shaped Norton source (Sin = Sv*|Yout|^2) plus PSRR path — matches measured loop-shaped, non-white output noise; flicker via Lorentzians. Discrete spurs: injected as deterministic vout tones (amplitude/phase from transient-FFT characterization). PSS/HB: excellent IF passivity/causality/min-phase are enforced and the block is the linear subcircuit feeding the nonlinear core — this is the most HB-convergence-friendly representation of an LDO supply, and the directly relevant approach for fast PSS/HB spur fidelity.

methods.name: Circuit-envelope and behavioral data-table models in HB (convergence-engineered behavioral blocks)
methods.what: General class of equation-defined / Verilog-A / data-table behavioral blocks consumed by HB and Circuit Envelope, with explicit attention to making them HB-convergent: ensuring C1/C2 continuity of nonlinear I-V/Q-V and of table interpolation, keeping each behavioral node connected to a linear element (so HB's linear/nonlinear partition succeeds), and using Krylov solvers, source/oversampling stepping, and adaptive nonlinearity/level ramping for convergence. Circuit Envelope runs the RF carrier in HB while the slow envelope (and supply modulation) advances in time — ideal when supply ripple is slow vs carrier.
methods.where_used: Keysight ADS (HB + Circuit Envelope, Genesys Harbec Krylov/APFT solvers, adaptive convergence), Qucs equation-defined/Verilog-A HB models (Brinson), Cadence Spectre HB/Envelope. Standard practice for stitching behavioral blocks into RF flows.
strengths: Directly addresses the central risk: behavioral-block HB convergence — guidance on continuity, partitioning, linear-element-per-node, and solver settings
Circuit Envelope is the natural home for 'slow supply ripple modulating a fast carrier' — efficient and convergent for supply-induced sidebands
Accepts the rational-fit LDO macromodel, X-params, and noise/spur sources together in one analysis
weaknesses: Convergence is only as good as the block's smoothness — table/Verilog-A discontinuities, hard limiters (current limit, dropout) readily break HB
Envelope assumes timescale separation (envelope << carrier); fast/wideband supply transients near the carrier violate this
Tuning solver knobs (steadyratio, tstab, oversample, source-step) is expert work and circuit-specific
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f)/PSRR(f): carried by whatever linear sub-block (rational fit / S-params) you embed. Noise PSD: via cyclostationary noise of the envelope/HB engine or injected PSD sources. Discrete spurs: handled as additional envelope/baseband tones modulating the supply, or as injected sources; Circuit Envelope reproduces the resulting carrier sidebands efficiently. PSS/HB convergence: this family IS the convergence-engineering layer — its whole point is making behavioral supply blocks converge robustly in HB/Envelope (smoothness, partitioning, solver strategy).

findings.relevance_to_spur_pss: High but asymmetric. The single most important transfer from this family is conceptual and is the hot-S / X_S-X_T (PHD) result: a small supply-rail tone at fc+/-fd scatters around the RF carrier into COUPLED upper/lower sidebands, and you need BOTH the direct term (S / X_S, acting on the ripple a) and the conjugate term (T / X_T, acting on conj(a)) to get sideband amplitude AND relative phase/asymmetry right. That is exactly what Cadence PAC/PXF compute and what the project must reproduce. However, the full X-parameter/PHD machinery is overkill (and wrong-shaped) for an LDO-as-supply: standard X-params reference a single large RF tone and treat supply only as a bias dimension, so they bloat the model and add table-smoothness convergence risk without modeling Zout(f)/PSRR(f)/noise the way a supply element needs. The directly applicable, fast, convergent approach is the rational-fit (vector-fitting) multiport behavioral macromodel of the LDO itself — Zout(f) + PSRR(f) + control transfer as low-order passive/causal pole-residue blocks, plus a loop-shaped Norton noise source and deterministic spur tones injected at vout — which is precisely the architecture the project converged on. The project's own findings (spur band is linear to ~-62 dBc; transient governed by Zout(s); noise loop-shaped not white; spurs deterministic) confirm that a linear-multiport-plus-injected-spurs model is sufficient in the near-carrier small-perturbation regime, and the X_S/X_T insight tells you to keep PSRR a signed/phase-correct (sectioned) transfer so sideband phase/asymmetry is right. For HB/PSS convergence the key levers are: enforce passivity/causality and avoid the non-minimum-phase artifacts vector fitting can introduce; keep the block C1/C2-continuous; ensure every behavioral node touches a linear element so HB partitioning succeeds; and beware the documented SpectreRF shooting-Pnoise caveat that low-frequency supply-noise upconversion can be under-reported at large-time-constant supply nodes (mirror the slow LDO pole carefully). Where the supply ripple is large or the LDO enters compression/current-limit/dropout, escalate from the linear macromodel to a memory-augmented / envelope (large-signal) supply-input model.

findings.category: Machine-learning / data-driven surrogate models for analog/RF/PMIC circuits (NN behavioral models, RNN/state-space NN, neural-ODE, GP/PCE surrogates, ANN macromodels in Verilog-A, ML-in-EDA), assessed for LDO-as-supply-element spur fidelity in PSS/HB

methods.name: Continuous-time RNN (CTRNN) behavioral model exported to Verilog-A (Chen/Raginsky/Rosenbaum lineage)
methods.what: Data-driven time-domain behavioral model where a discrete-time RNN is fit to input/output port trajectories (node voltages and currents) of a transistor-level IP block, then transformed into a continuous-time RNN (CTRNN) - a set of first-order nonlinear ODEs dx/dt=f(x,u), y=h(x) - and emitted as a Verilog-A module so a commercial simulator integrates it with its own adaptive timestep. The continuous-time form is the key trick: it decouples the learned model from a fixed training timestep so the SPICE/Spectre solver can choose its own steps and interconnect the block with arbitrary loads. This is the canonical 'replace an IP block with a cheap learned macromodel' approach for SoC verification.
methods.where_used: Academic, University of Illinois (Zaichen Chen, Maxim Raginsky, Elyse Rosenbaum), EPEPS 2017; influential reference architecture cited by nearly all later Verilog-A NN work. Targeted at SoC/IP-block transient verification (I/O drivers, ESD, analog/RF IPs). Not a vendor product but the de-facto template for ANN-in-Verilog-A.
strengths: Verilog-A export means it drops into Spectre/SPICE and composes with surrounding circuitry - exactly the deployment path an LDO supply macromodel needs
Continuous-time ODE form is compatible with variable-timestep solvers (avoids the fixed-step trap of discrete RNN/NARX)
Learns purely from port waveforms, no need for the netlist/IP internals (good for encrypted/foundry IP)
Captures nonlinear dynamic memory effects (slew, recovery) that a static LUT or pure LTI Zout/PSRR misses
weaknesses: Trained and validated in the time domain only; no demonstrated PSS/HB use, and a learned nonlinear ODE is not guaranteed to converge a shooting/HB Jacobian or to be passive
Plain CTRNN has no stability guarantee - can blow up or fail to converge when interconnected with an unseen load (the exact failure ISS-NODE was created to fix)
Nothing about frequency-domain Zout(f)/PSRR(f) fidelity, noise PSD, or discrete spurs - these are not in the training objective, so they are accidental at best
Spur fidelity near a carrier requires sub-microvolt port accuracy across many decades of frequency; a waveform-MSE-trained RNN will not hit that without targeting it
methods.supply_handling: Handles supply pin as just another input port if you train on (vsupply, iload -> vout) trajectories. In principle the model's small-signal Jacobian along an operating trajectory yields Zout and a vsupply->vout transfer (PSRR), but the literature does NOT verify those against an AC reference. Noise PSD: not modeled (deterministic ODE, no stochastic source). Discrete spurs: only reproducible if excited in training data and the RNN happens to capture the tone - not guaranteed and not a design target. PSS/HB: no published PSS/HB results; convergence robustness under shooting/HB is unproven and the deterministic ODE has no provision for periodic-steady-state spectral accuracy a spur study demands.
methods.maturity: emerging (academic). Verilog-A path is real and reusable; spur/PSS use is not demonstrated.

methods.name: Input-to-State-Stable Neural ODE (ISS-NODE) for transient circuit macromodels
methods.what: A neural-ODE/CTRNN whose dynamics are parametrized so they are provably input-to-state stable (ISS) with respect to a jointly-learned Lur'e-Postnikov (quadratic-plus-integral) Lyapunov function. ISS guarantees the learned state stays bounded for bounded input and returns to a unique equilibrium at zero input - an inductive bias that makes the macromodel behave when embedded in feedback with unknown loads in a commercial simulator. It directly attacks the #1 practical failure of learned dynamics: a model that validates well but diverges or fails to converge when interconnected at test time with a load not seen in training. Also shown to learn ISS-preserving perturbations for aging/degradation.
methods.where_used: Academic, University of Illinois (Alan Yang, Jie Xiong, Maxim Raginsky, Elyse Rosenbaum), arXiv:2202.06453, 2022 (L4DC). Same group as the Verilog-A CTRNN work; explicitly motivated by SoC IP-block macromodels simulated in commercial circuit simulators.
strengths: Provable stability/boundedness is the single most important property for simulator convergence and robustness to unseen loads - exactly the LDO-as-supply-element use case (model sees many different RF loads/decoupling networks)
Builds on the Verilog-A-exportable CTRNN form, so the deployment path is intact
Lyapunov constraint improves generalization in novel interconnection configurations (validated by interconnecting with components not seen in training)
Stability is enforced by construction, not by soft regularization, so it holds everywhere not just on the training set
weaknesses: ISS guarantees transient boundedness/convergence to equilibrium, NOT periodic-steady-state existence/uniqueness - it does not by itself certify HB/PSS Jacobian convergence around a periodic orbit driven by a strong RF tone
Still a deterministic ODE: no native noise PSD, no stochastic spur mechanism
Demonstrated on digital/analog transient + aging; no Zout(f)/PSRR(f)/spur validation
ISS is a sufficient inductive bias but can over-damp; an LDO's lightly-damped Zout resonance and supply ripple feedthrough sit near the boundary the constraint pushes away from
methods.supply_handling: Same port-based handling as the CTRNN; supply enters as an input. Its real contribution to the spur/PSS goal is robustness/convergence: an ISS macromodel is far less likely to break a shooting/HB solve than an unconstrained RNN. But it gives no specific guarantee on spectral spur accuracy, noise PSD, or PSRR(f) shape - those remain a function of the (time-domain only) training data and loss. For a spur study you would still need to (a) verify the linearization gives correct Zout(f)/PSRR(f) and (b) add a noise/spur mechanism separately.
methods.maturity: emerging (academic). The most relevant single idea for convergence robustness; spectral spur fidelity unproven.

methods.name: Hybrid Neural-ODE + RNN (NODE-RNN / NCDE-RNN) continuous-time macromodels in Verilog-A
methods.what: A 2025 evolution that fuses continuous-time neural differential equations (Neural ODE, and Neural Controlled Differential Equations) with a discrete RNN update at observation points: the neural ODE evolves the hidden state continuously between samples while the RNN injects discrete-event corrections, trained with a hybrid adjoint method. Explicitly framed against TDRNN/NARX, whose fixed timestep and output-feedback cause 'serious simulation accuracy and convergence issues' and instability under a variable-step SPICE solver. Deployed to Verilog-A and validated in circuit emulation. Reported +33% fit over plain NCDE and +24% over CTRNN, validated on PIN diodes in high-power microwave environments capturing nonlinear memory effects.
methods.where_used: Academic (Zenghui Chang, Yang Zhang, Hu Tan, Hong Cai Chen, Southeast University), arXiv:2503.22313, 2025. Aimed at high-precision time-domain modeling of circuit IPs and devices (RF amplifiers, drivers, ESD, here PIN diodes).
strengths: Best-in-class statement of WHY discrete RNN/NARX break SPICE convergence (fixed step vs adaptive step, output feedback instability) - directly informs why a spur/PSS model must be continuous-time and stable
Continuous-time core + Verilog-A deployment + adaptive-step compatibility, with measured accuracy gains over CTRNN/NCDE
Captures strong nonlinear memory (validated on high-power microwave PIN diode), relevant if LDO drive nonlinearity matters
NCDE form handles irregular/continuous control inputs cleanly
weaknesses: Still purely transient/time-domain; no PSS/HB results and no stability proof (unlike ISS-NODE) - the hybrid discrete-update step could itself introduce non-smoothness that hurts a Newton-based HB/shooting Jacobian
No frequency-domain Zout/PSRR validation, no noise PSD, no spur spectrum analysis
Hybrid adjoint training is heavier; reproducibility/tooling immature
Validation on a two-terminal device, not a multiport supply element with a feedback loop
methods.supply_handling: Optimized for transient nonlinear memory, not the small-signal frequency-domain quantities a supply-element spur model needs. Zout(f)/PSRR(f) would again come only from linearizing the learned ODE (unverified). No noise, no explicit spur mechanism. The discrete-update component is a potential convergence liability under HB/PSS Newton iterations even though it helps transient accuracy.
methods.maturity: emerging/academic-only. State-of-the-art for transient fidelity; not validated for the spectral/PSS goal.

methods.name: State-Space Dynamic Neural Network (SSDNN) with provable stability (Zhang/Cao lineage)
methods.what: An earlier, foundational state-space neural-network macromodel for high-speed nonlinear circuits/IC buffers: learns a generalized dynamic NN in state-space form from transient I/O waveforms using adjoint sensitivity, then - critically - derives explicit LOCAL and GLOBAL stability criteria expressed as eigenvalue tests on stability matrices built from the trained internal weights. A trained model can be checked for stability post-hoc and implemented in SPICE-like simulators via an exact circuit representation. This is the original 'NN macromodel you can certify stable and put in a simulator' work and the conceptual ancestor of CTRNN/ISS-NODE. The same group also produced neuro-transfer-function / parametric NN-plus-transfer-function frequency-domain models.
methods.where_used: Academic, classic in the IEEE MTT / microwave-CAD community (Y. Cao, R. Ding, Q.-J. Zhang, IEEE T-MTT 2006; related IBIS/IC-buffer modeling). Widely cited in RF/microwave behavioral-modeling and signal-integrity contexts.
strengths: Stability is verifiable via eigenvalue tests - predates and motivates ISS-NODE; good for simulator robustness
State-space form is naturally simulator-implementable and composable
Mature, peer-reviewed, validated on high-speed IC buffers (a supply/IO context)
Companion Zhang-group frequency-domain neuro-TF models DO target S-parameters/Zin/Zout, useful to pair with the time-domain SSDNN
weaknesses: Stability criteria are for the autonomous/forced transient response, not a guarantee of HB/PSS periodic-orbit convergence
Vintage method; tooling and training are dated vs modern autodiff neural-ODE stacks
Time-domain SSDNN itself does not directly deliver calibrated Zout(f)/PSRR(f); you use the companion frequency-domain NN-TF models for that
No noise PSD or spur generation; deterministic
methods.supply_handling: The SSDNN time-domain model gives transient supply response and, via linearization, an approximate Zout/PSRR. The companion Zhang-group neuro-transfer-function models are the part that actually fits frequency-domain Zout(f)/PSRR(f)/S-parameters with passivity/stability enforcement - that pairing is the most directly relevant classical route to the LTI multiport (Zout+PSRR) part of an LDO supply model, which mirrors the user's current physics-based per-block approach. Discrete spurs and noise PSD are out of scope for both. PSS/HB: the frequency-domain TF form is HB-friendly (it IS a frequency response), but the nonlinear SSDNN's PSS convergence is not characterized.
methods.maturity: mainstream-production in RF/microwave CAD for behavioral macromodeling (peer-reviewed, decades of use); but not for spur/PSS specifically.

methods.name: ANN large-signal device/macromodel in Verilog-A, validated in Harmonic Balance (GaN-HEMT lineage, Jarndal et al.)
methods.what: An artificial neural network (typically MLP / cascade-feedforward) trained on DC-IV and RF large-signal data to represent a device's nonlinear constitutive relations (charges/currents), coded into Verilog-A and run inside the simulator's transient, HARMONIC BALANCE, and envelope engines. This is the family that has actually been exercised under HB and reports convergence and spectral accuracy: ANN GaN-HEMT models validated by single- and two-tone large-signal RF measurements with 'perfect fitting and high convergence rates,' and ~5x faster than table-based models.
methods.where_used: Academic + practitioner RF/PA community; commercial-tool-adjacent (Keysight ADS, Cadence). A. Jarndal et al. (Verilog-A-based ANN GaN-HEMT, IEEE 2024; 'Improved Verilog-A Based ANN Modeling Applied to GaN HEMTs,' Advanced Theory and Simulations / Wiley 2025). Earlier ANN-HEMT-in-ADS work (Microwave Journal). Used for power-amplifier design.
strengths: Actually demonstrated to CONVERGE in Harmonic Balance and reproduce single-/two-tone spectra and intermodulation - the only family with real HB spur/IMD validation
Verilog-A, drops into ADS/Spectre HB/PSS, faster than LUT models
Because it models smooth charge/current constitutive functions (with continuous derivatives), the HB Jacobian is well-behaved - the right recipe for convergence
Two-tone IMD = sidebands/spurs around a carrier, which is exactly the spectral quantity an LDO spur study cares about (proof the approach can render spurs in HB)
weaknesses: It is a DEVICE/PA model, not a supply-element/regulator model - you would have to recast the LDO as a learned nonlinear two/three-port with a supply pin
Spurs here are deterministic distortion products of the drive; it does not model an LDO's own noise PSD or internally-generated tones (e.g., from a switching reference/charge pump)
Smoothness/derivative continuity of the ANN must be engineered (activation choice, no kinks) or HB convergence degrades - a real practical risk
PSRR(f)/Zout(f) accuracy is only as good as the bias/load coverage of the training set; not a stated objective
methods.supply_handling: Strongest existing evidence that a Verilog-A ANN can produce CORRECT, CONVERGENT spectra (IMD/harmonics) under HB - i.e., it can render carrier sidebands. Mapping to LDO: model the pass device + loop as smooth nonlinear constitutive functions of (vsupply, vout, iload); the linearization then yields Zout(f) and PSRR(f) automatically, and large-signal supply ripple feedthrough produces sidebands in HB. Noise PSD and stochastic/deterministic intrinsic spurs are NOT covered and must be added by a separate mechanism (noise sources / explicit tone injection). Distortion-of-an-applied-tone spurs ARE in scope; spurs intrinsic to the LDO are not.
methods.maturity: mainstream-production-adjacent for RF device/PA modeling (shipped in ADS/Spectre HB flows); not yet applied to LDO supply spur modeling.

methods.name: Neural-network-assisted / NN-based Harmonic Balance solvers
methods.what: Instead of (or alongside) being the device model, a neural network is used to accelerate or replace the HB solve itself: a back-propagation NN is trained so its outputs are the steady-state harmonic coefficients that satisfy the nonlinear circuit's HB governing equations, with a convergence theorem showing the training can reach the global minimum. Related work uses augmented NNs with explicit periodic units (plus RNNs) to learn the nonlinear time-domain steady-state of oscillators-with-buffers, i.e., learning the periodic steady state directly.
methods.where_used: Academic ('A Neural Network-Based Harmonic Balance Technique for Analysis of Periodic Responses of Nonlinear Circuits,' Circuits Systems and Signal Processing, Springer 2022; Penn State 'Behavioral Modeling of Steady-State Oscillators with Buffers Using Neural Networks'). RF/microwave numerical-methods community.
strengths: Directly targets the PERIODIC STEADY STATE and its harmonic spectrum - conceptually aligned with PSS/HB spur output
The AugNN/periodic-unit idea explicitly encodes periodicity, so it represents tones/sidebands natively rather than hoping a generic RNN learns a limit cycle
Can serve as a fast initial guess / homotopy aid to improve HB convergence of a hard nonlinear block
weaknesses: These replace or augment the solver; they are not drop-in Verilog-A behavioral models you embed in a commercial PSS/HB run of a larger RF system
Convergence theorems are for the NN training, not for a commercial-tool HB Newton iteration around the learned block
Demonstrated on small/canonical nonlinear circuits and oscillators, not multiport LDO-as-supply with calibrated Zout/PSRR/noise
No noise PSD; spur fidelity to sub-mdBc not shown
methods.supply_handling: Most natural fit conceptually to a spur/sideband objective because it works in the harmonic domain, but practically it operates at the solver/algorithm level rather than as a supply-element macromodel, so it does not directly give a reusable Zout(f)/PSRR(f)/noise LDO block for someone else's PSS deck. Could be valuable as a convergence accelerator (good periodic-orbit initial guess) for whatever LDO model you do embed.

methods.name: HB surrogate ANN immunity macromodel of a voltage regulator (DPI/EMC)
methods.what: A surrogate ANN (multi-hidden-layer MLP) behavioral model of a voltage regulator built specifically to run inside Harmonic Balance and predict the regulator's response to RF disturbance injected on its pins (Direct RF Power Injection / conducted-immunity, including continuous-wave and amplitude-modulated disturbance). The surrogate replaces the transistor-level regulator to cut HB simulation time and conceal IP, and reproduces the device's nonlinear response under large RF disturbance - i.e., it predicts how an injected RF tone perturbs the regulator's DC/spectrum.
methods.where_used: Academic + industrial EMC (IEEE T-EMC; industrial automotive voltage-regulator case study). The closest published analog to 'model a regulator as a black box inside HB and see what an RF tone does to it.'
strengths: It is literally a voltage regulator modeled as an ANN surrogate running in HARMONIC BALANCE under an injected RF tone - the most on-point precedent for an LDO-near-carrier HB study
Handles both CW and amplitude-modulated disturbance, i.e., sidebands/envelope effects around the injected tone
Designed to reduce HB sim time and hide IP, which is the practical motivation for a behavioral LDO supply model
Demonstrates ANN+HB convergence for a regulator-class block
weaknesses: Goal is EMC immunity (how external RF upsets the regulator), not high-fidelity Zout(f)/PSRR(f)/noise of the regulator as a clean supply - accuracy targets are coarser than spur-fidelity (dBc) work
No noise PSD modeling and no intrinsic-spur modeling; spurs are intermod of the injected disturbance
Older surrogate-ANN tech; static MLP mapping in the HB harmonic domain may miss fine memory/loop dynamics
Fidelity to the sub-mdBc spur levels a supply study often needs is not demonstrated
methods.supply_handling: Directly addresses supply-pin RF injection in HB and produces the resulting spectrum/sidebands - so it proves the workflow (ANN regulator macromodel + HB + RF tone -> spectral response) is feasible and convergent. But it is tuned for large-disturbance immunity, not for accurate small-signal Zout(f)/PSRR(f), output noise PSD, or low-level discrete spurs. Use it as the workflow template; you would need a much tighter accuracy spec and added noise/spur mechanisms for true spur fidelity.
methods.maturity: emerging (peer-reviewed, with an industrial case study); proven in HB but for immunity, not spur-fidelity supply modeling.

methods.name: Gaussian-Process (GP) and Polynomial-Chaos-Expansion (PCE) surrogates (incl. PCE-GP hybrids, stochastic testing)
methods.what: Statistical/spectral surrogates that map design or process-variation parameters to circuit performance metrics (gain, bandwidth, PSRR value, transient settling, ripple, yield). GP gives a smooth interpolant with calibrated uncertainty; generalized PCE / stochastic testing expands the response in orthogonal polynomials of random parameters for fast uncertainty quantification with provable convergence when parameter count is small/medium; PCE-GP hybrids (PCEGP) use PCE to set input-dependent GP hyperparameters for non-stationary/heteroscedastic responses. Used to replace expensive SPICE Monte Carlo for yield/variability and for Bayesian optimization of analog sizing.
methods.where_used: Academic + EDA-research (Z. Zhang / L. Daniel MIT stochastic testing; broad UQ-for-EDA literature; PoBO for photonic-IC yield; PCEGP 2024). Used in design-space exploration, yield-aware optimization, and variability analysis - not in the inner simulation loop.
strengths: Excellent for parameter->scalar-metric surrogates (e.g., predict PSRR-at-1MHz or settling time vs corner) with uncertainty bars; huge speedup over Monte Carlo for UQ/yield
PCE has rigorous convergence theory for low/medium parameter dimensionality
Cheap to evaluate, smooth, good for Bayesian optimization of LDO sizing and for sensitivity analysis
weaknesses: These are STATIC input->output regressors over parameters, NOT dynamical simulator-embeddable blocks - they cannot be placed in a netlist and run in PSS/HB
Do not represent Zout(f)/PSRR(f) as transfer functions, do not generate spectra, do not produce noise PSD or spurs; they predict scalar/curve metrics, not a port behavior the solver iterates on
Curse of dimensionality: PCE blows up with many parameters; GP scales poorly with large datasets
No notion of convergence inside an RF simulator because they are never inside one
methods.supply_handling: Essentially orthogonal to the spur/PSS goal. They can predict a PSRR number, a noise-integrated value, or yield vs corners as a surrogate over design/process knobs, and are very useful for choosing/optimizing an LDO design or for fast corner exploration of your behavioral model's fitted coefficients. But they do NOT provide a Zout(f)/PSRR(f) port, do not emit spurs, do not give a noise PSD usable by a noise analysis, and cannot be embedded in PSS/HB. Treat them as an outer-loop optimization/UQ tool wrapping a real (physics or NN-ODE) behavioral model, not as the supply element itself.
methods.maturity: mainstream-production for UQ/yield/optimization (well-established); not applicable as an in-simulator spur/PSS model.

methods.name: Graph-Neural-Network / DNN performance predictors for analog/RF design-space exploration (and analog surrogate frameworks, e.g., LASANA)
methods.what: GNNs that ingest a circuit netlist as a graph and predict performance metrics (gain, noise figure, bandwidth, S-parameter-derived figures) data-efficiently across topologies; and broader analog surrogate frameworks (e.g., LASANA, 2025) that learn block-level output/state/energy/latency to plug into architectural simulators. The aim is fast performance prediction or system-level co-simulation, not transistor-accurate port behavior in an RF solver.
methods.where_used: Academic (RF-informed GNN, Asadi/Partin-Vaisband, arXiv:2508.16403 2025; LASANA, Ho/Gerstlauer UT Austin, arXiv:2507.10748 2025), and the active ML-for-EDA wave that EDA vendors (Cadence, Synopsys, Siemens) are commercializing for sizing/layout/optimization. Design-space exploration and architecture-level simulation.
strengths: Data-efficient, generalizes across topologies; great for early design exploration and ranking candidate LDO architectures
GNN respects circuit topology, so it can predict frequency-domain figures from structure
LASANA-style learned block models speed up large system simulation by orders of magnitude
weaknesses: Predict performance metrics / coarse block behavior, NOT a transistor-accurate, simulator-embeddable supply port - explicitly design-space-exploration tools, not PSS/HB blocks
No Zout(f)/PSRR(f) transfer-function fidelity at the level needed for spurs, no noise PSD, no discrete spur generation
Accuracy is statistical/aggregate; spur fidelity (dBc) is far outside their stated scope
No convergence story inside an RF simulator because they live above it
methods.supply_handling: Not relevant as a supply element in PSS/HB. Useful only at the periphery: a GNN/DNN could pick or pre-size an LDO architecture, or LASANA-style models could carry an LDO's coarse behavior in a system-level (non-RF) sim. They do not model Zout(f), PSRR(f), noise PSD, or spurs at spur-study fidelity and are not embeddable in PSS/HB.
methods.maturity: emerging (research), with the ML-in-EDA optimization/exploration subset moving into production tools - but the spur/PSS supply-modeling use is out of scope.

findings.relevance_to_spur_pss: Moderately relevant as a family, with one clear winner pattern and a lot of noise. The directly useful thread is the continuous-time NN-ODE-in-Verilog-A lineage (Chen 2017 CTRNN -> Yang 2022 ISS-NODE -> Chang 2025 NODE-RNN/NCDE-RNN) plus the ANN-large-signal-in-Verilog-A device family that is actually proven to CONVERGE in Harmonic Balance and render single/two-tone IMD spectra (Jarndal GaN-HEMT), and the HB-surrogate ANN voltage-regulator immunity model - the closest published precedent for 'regulator-as-black-box in HB under an RF tone.' These show the deployment path (Verilog-A), the convergence prerequisites (continuous-time + smooth derivatives + provable stability/ISS), and a real HB spectral result. However, NONE of them targets the trio the user cares about - calibrated Zout(f), PSRR(f), output noise PSD, and low-level DISCRETE spurs at dBc fidelity. NN behavioral models are deterministic ODEs, so noise PSD and intrinsic spurs are not modeled at all and must be bolted on separately; spurs only appear as distortion of an applied tone. GP/PCE and GNN/DNN surrogates are essentially irrelevant to the in-simulator spur/PSS task: they are outer-loop parameter->metric regressors for yield/optimization/exploration, not simulator-embeddable supply ports. Critically for spur fidelity, a learned nonlinear ODE puts the model's Zout/PSRR/spur content at the mercy of its training loss and Jacobian smoothness; without explicitly enforcing (a) correct small-signal frequency response, (b) passivity/stability for HB convergence, and (c) a separate stochastic/deterministic noise+spur mechanism, a generic ML surrogate will not beat the user's current physics-based per-block approach (LTI Zout+PSRR multiport + shaped Norton noise + explicit deterministic spur tones), which already achieves dBc-level fidelity by construction and is inherently HB/PSS-friendly. Net: the ML family contributes the convergence-robustness ideas (ISS / continuous-time / smooth derivatives) and the Verilog-A-in-HB deployment template, and is worth borrowing from for any nonlinear extension (slew, gm compression), but is NOT a turnkey replacement for the linear+spur+noise decomposition when the objective is fast, convergent, low-level spur fidelity near an RF carrier.
sources: Z. Chen, M. Raginsky, E. Rosenbaum, 'Verilog-A compatible recurrent neural network model for transient circuit simulation,' IEEE EPEPS 2017 (DOI 10.1109/EPEPS.2017.8329743); https://experts.illinois.edu/en/publications/verilog-a-compatible-recurrent-neural-network-model-for-transient

findings.category: Power-Integrity / Power-Delivery-Network (PDN) modeling of voltage regulators and PMICs

methods.name: Closed-loop output-impedance Zout(f) one-port (VRM/LDO as a 1-port for PDN co-design)
methods.what: The dominant PI view: model the regulator as a single 1-port frequency-dependent output impedance Zout(s)=Vout(s)/Iload(s) seen at the rail. Below the control-loop bandwidth Zout is forced low/near-zero by feedback; near loop crossover it peaks (anti-resonance, set by loop phase margin / Q); above loop bandwidth it is dictated by the passive PDN (Cout ESR/ESL then inductive +20 dB/dec). The regulator's Zout is then composed with package/board/decap impedances and the goal is a FLAT, low-Q target impedance Ztarget = Vripple_allowed / Itransient. Voltage ripple = Zpdn(f) x Iload(f); a flatter/lower-Q PDN yields lower ripple, jitter, EMI and better transient response. This is exactly the 'supply-as-impedance' abstraction.
methods.where_used: Mainstream board/package PI engineering. Keysight Power Integrity Boot Camp (Heidi Barnes), PathWave ADS PI flows, Cadence Sigrity/PowerSI/OptimizePI, Ansys SIwave/HFSS-PI, Bode-100/VNA-based VRM characterization. Microwave Journal 'Signal Integrity to Power Integrity' series, Signal Integrity Journal flat-impedance articles.
strengths: Single physically-meaningful transfer function captures load-step/transient behavior and the anti-resonance that dominates ringing
Composes cleanly with passive decap/package/board impedance (just series/parallel of 1-ports)
Directly measurable on silicon/board with a VNA (shunt-through), so model is validatable
Maps naturally to the user's project finding that transient is governed by Zout(s) and that the spur band is linear-multiport
weaknesses: Pure 1-port Zout says nothing about supply->output transfer (PSRR) — needs a second port/path
Single-port small-signal model omits large-signal compression/slew (valid only for small load perturbations, as the project already found ~50uA linear limit)
Captures noise/ripple only as a response to load current, not as an independent rail noise source
Q/anti-resonance is sensitive to operating point (Iload-dependent gm, Cout ESR), so one fixed Zout(f) may not generalize across bias
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): this IS the method's core deliverable; the loop-shaped peak and the +20dB/dec inductive tail are exactly reproduced. PSRR(f): NOT covered by a 1-port; requires upgrading to a 2-port (Vin->Vout path). Noise PSD: only the response part (Vn = Zout x In_load); intrinsic regulator noise must be added separately. Discrete spurs: not represented unless an explicit current/voltage tone is injected at the port. PSS/HB: a measured Zout must be turned into a rational/pole-residue model (next method) before it is HB/PSS-usable; raw tabular Zout is fine for AC but not directly for periodic-steady-state nonlinear solvers.

methods.name: Two-port / multiport behavioral macromodel (Zout + PSRR + intrinsic noise as a small-signal N-port)
methods.what: Upgrade the 1-port to a small-signal 2-port (or N-port) of the rail: an output-impedance path Zout(f), a supply-rejection path PSRR(f)=Vout/Vin (or its inverse, line transfer), and an intrinsic output-referred noise source. This is the 'supply = impedance + noise + ripple' triplet. The PSRR path lets an upstream supply tone/ripple appear at the regulated output; the noise source carries the regulator's own broadband + flicker PSD; ripple/spurs are injected as deterministic sources. This is precisely the architecture the user's project converged on (per-block swappable Zout + signed-section PSRR + Norton-at-vout noise + deterministic spur tones).
methods.where_used: On-die/SoC PI and AMS modeling; appears in PSIJ literature (LDO modeled by its PSRR response), Keysight ADS measurement-based VRM 2-port models, and EMI terminal models (3-terminal Norton). Encoded as Verilog-A/Verilog-AMS or SPICE behavioral (ABM/E,G,H sources) blocks. The user's repo (variants.py / per-block model + selector).
strengths: Separates the three physical mechanisms (load->out, supply->out, intrinsic noise) so each can be fit/validated independently and swapped per architecture
PSRR path is the right primitive for supply-induced sidebands around a carrier
Linear/multiport when nonlinearity is negligible (matches the project's <-62 dBc spur finding) -> very fast, very convergent in HB/PSS
Output-referred shaped-Norton noise (Sin = Sv x |Yout|^2) correctly reproduces loop-shaped, NOT white-through-Zout, noise
weaknesses: Each transfer (Zout, PSRR) must itself be a stable, ideally passive rational model or HB/transient can misbehave
Cross-terms (load-dependent PSRR, intermod between supply tone and load) are dropped in a purely linear 2-port
PSRR is often non-minimum-phase / sign-changing across frequency (the project's V4 difficulty) — naive magnitude-only fits give wrong sideband phase
Validation needs both AC (Zout, PSRR) and PSS/Pnoise (noise, spurs), i.e. a multi-analysis harness
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): explicit 1-port path. PSRR(f): explicit Vin->Vout path — the key enabler for supply-tone sidebands; must preserve phase/sign to get sideband asymmetry right. Noise PSD: explicit output-referred Norton (white + Lorentzians/flicker), decoupled from Zout so it survives load changes. Discrete spurs: injected as deterministic tones at the output node (the project's transient-FFT-fit spur block). PSS/HB: if every path is a low-order rational/synthesized-RLC block, the model is small, smooth and strongly convergent under HB/PSS/shooting; it adds essentially no stiffness because there's no internal switching/regulation loop to converge.

methods.name: Rational/pole-residue macromodeling: Vector Fitting (+ passivity enforcement) and equivalent-circuit synthesis
methods.what: Fit measured/simulated frequency-domain data (Zout, PSRR, or full Y/Z/S of the rail) to a stable pole-residue rational function H(s)=sum_k r_k/(s-p_k)+d+s*e, with stable poles (real or conjugate pairs). Passivity is verified (Hamiltonian-matrix eigenvalues / half-size test) and enforced (residue-matrix eigenvalue perturbation). The rational model is then realized either as an equivalent RLC/controlled-source SPICE subcircuit (Foster/Cauer-style synthesis) or as a state-space block, or interfaced by recursive convolution / Norton equivalent in transient. This is the bridge that turns a tabular Zout/PSRR into something a nonlinear PSS/HB solver can use.
methods.where_used: Gustavsen & Semlyen Vector Fitting (vectfit3, SINTEF Matrix Fitting Toolbox); standard in PI/SI macromodeling, IdEM/Idemworks, Cadence/ADS broadband SPICE model generation, power-grid and interconnect ROM. Widely taught (e.g. NTU PI macromodeling course).
strengths: Turns black-box measured Zout/PSRR into a compact, stable, causal model usable in time- and frequency-domain solvers
Passivity enforcement guarantees stable transient/HB behavior when connected to active loads
Low order -> fast and convergent; orders of magnitude smaller than tabular convolution
Handles Y/Z/S equally; can fit the non-minimum-phase PSRR if real+imag (complex) data are fit (not magnitude only)
weaknesses: Passivity enforcement can perturb the fit (accuracy/passivity tradeoff), and for a 1-port noise/PSRR path 'passivity' may be the wrong constraint (PSRR is not a passive immittance, so naive passivity enforcement is inappropriate — must enforce only stability/causality there)
Magnitude-only fitting loses phase -> wrong sideband phase/asymmetry; needs complex data
Pole count and expansion choice are heuristic; over-fitting measurement noise creates spurious resonances that become spurious HB tones
Equivalent-circuit synthesis of complex residues needs controlled sources, which some HB engines handle better than others
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): fitted directly; synthesized RLC is HB/PSS-friendly. PSRR(f): fit as a transfer function (stability+causality, NOT passivity); preserves phase for correct sidebands. Noise PSD: the shaped noise source can be implemented as white noise filtered by a fitted rational shaping filter. Discrete spurs: not produced by the rational model itself (it's LTI) — spurs come from injected sources, but the rational Zout correctly shapes/colors how an injected tone appears at the port. PSS/HB: this is the method that makes the others HB-ready — a low-order passive rational block is smooth, has no stiff dynamics, and converges fast; tabular-S convolution by contrast is slow and can be non-causal/non-convergent.

methods.name: Reduced-order modeling (MOR) of the PDN: Krylov/moment-matching (PRIMA, SPRIM, PVL)
methods.what: Projection-based model-order reduction of the (large) linear RLC PDN/power-grid: build a Krylov subspace via block Arnoldi/Lanczos at an expansion point, congruence-project the MNA matrices to a small order q model that matches the first q moments of the transfer function. PRIMA guarantees passivity (and SPRIM additionally preserves block structure/reciprocity and matches twice as many moments for the same cost). Used to compress the passive network around/feeding the regulator (grid, decaps, package) into a compact N-port that the regulator model plugs into.
methods.where_used: EDA power-grid analysis and interconnect ROM (Cadence, Synopsys, Ansys back-ends); Pillage/Pileggi/Odabasioglu (PRIMA, ICCAD'97), Freund (PVL/SPRIM). Academic + production for chip-level PDN.
strengths: Massive compression of passive PDN while provably preserving passivity -> stable when coupled to active regulator/load
Accurate broadband near expansion point; multi-point variants extend bandwidth
Directly produces a small N-port that composes with the regulator's Zout/PSRR model
weaknesses: Reduces only the LINEAR passive network — the regulator's active loop, noise and spurs are NOT captured (must be added as the active 1/2-port)
Compression collapses with very large port count (power grids have thousands of ports)
Accuracy concentrated near expansion point; wideband needs higher order or multi-point
No notion of noise PSD or discrete spurs at all
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): the passive part of Zout (decaps, grid, package) is exactly what MOR compresses; combined with the active loop model it gives full Zout. PSRR(f): not modeled (linear passive only). Noise PSD: none. Discrete spurs: none. PSS/HB: the reduced passive N-port is small and passive -> excellent HB/PSS conditioning; it's the right way to keep the surrounding network from bloating the harmonic-balance Jacobian. It is complementary to (not a replacement for) the active regulator behavioral model.

methods.name: PMIC/switching-regulator behavioral models for transient: state-space averaging and SIMPLIS PWL/POP+AC
methods.what: For switched-mode supplies (and PMIC rails) two production approaches: (a) state-space / average models that hide switching for fast startup/transient/AC and optionally overlay synthetic ripple; (b) SIMPLIS (SIMulation for Piecewise-LInear System), which represents devices as PWL segments and the converter as a cyclic sequence of linear topologies, then uses Periodic Operating Point (POP) to find the switching steady-state limit cycle quickly and an AC-at-POP to extract small-signal frequency response (incl. Zout, control-to-output, and even PSRR) 10-50x faster than SPICE without deriving an average model.
methods.where_used: PMIC/DC-DC vendor models (TI, ADI, Infineon), SIMetrix/SIMPLIS, Keysight ADS state-space averaged VRM behavioral models, EMI/ripple system studies. Used to GENERATE the Zout(f)/control-loop data that then feeds the rational/1-port abstraction.
strengths: POP+AC gives accurate switching-aware Zout(f) and loop response without averaging approximations
Average models give very fast transient/startup for system PI
Captures the real switching ripple spur comb (fsw and harmonics) that a linear LDO model would miss
Bridges to the abstract models: run POP/AC to extract Zout/PSRR, then fit rational
weaknesses: SIMPLIS/PWL and switching transient are slow and NOT directly compatible with RF HB/PSS near a GHz carrier (huge timescale separation: fsw ~MHz vs carrier ~GHz)
PWL switching is the opposite of what you want inside a fast spur-fidelity model — too stiff/expensive
Average models drop the switching spurs that may matter for sidebands; ripple overlay is synthetic
Vendor encrypted models often won't converge in HB and can't be reduced
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): extracted via POP+AC (accurate, switching-aware). PSRR(f): line-to-output via AC-at-POP. Noise PSD: not its strength (deterministic ripple, not stochastic PSD). Discrete spurs: NATURALLY produced (fsw harmonics) — this is where switching spurs come from, but they are far below an RF carrier. PSS/HB: poor fit for fast RF spur modeling — switching PWL/POP is its own steady-state engine, not RF HB; the right use is OFFLINE characterization (extract Zout/PSRR/ripple spectrum) which then becomes injected tones + rational paths in the lightweight HB model.

methods.name: Black-box terminal EMI / conducted-emission models: 3-terminal Norton (impedances + noise sources)
methods.what: Frequency-domain black-box model of a converter/regulator as a small terminal network: typically 2-3 impedances plus 1-2 independent noise sources (Norton or Thevenin), extracted from terminal measurements, that reproduces the device's terminal impedance AND its emitted noise spectrum independent of source/load (via superposition). Common-mode and differential-mode are separated; CM source impedance is capacitive (falls with f), DM is resistive/inductive (rises with f). Modular-Terminal-Behavioral (MTB) variants superpose per-operating-zone frequency-domain sources to cover the whole period.
methods.where_used: Power-electronics EMI/EMC engineering and filter co-design; CPES (Virginia Tech), IET Power Electronics / IEEE TPEL behavioral EMI papers, TI 'Engineer's Guide to EMI in DC-DC' app notes. Realized in SPICE/LTspice or ADS from Touchstone.
strengths: Cleanly separates 'impedance' from 'noise source' — exactly the supply-as-(impedance+noise) abstraction, and explicitly carries a deterministic emission spectrum
Load/source independent (the whole point) -> reusable across PDN contexts
Directly measurement-extractable and validated against measured conducted emissions
Superposition makes multiple noise sources / spurs additive and easy to compose
weaknesses: Built for kHz-MHz conducted-EMI band, not GHz RF carriers; impedance extraction assumes LTI terminal behavior
Linear/superposition assumption breaks if the noise source impedance is operating-point dependent
Phase of the noise source (needed for sideband asymmetry) is often under-specified vs magnitude
Not a loop/PSRR model — it's emission-centric, so supply-rejection of an upstream tone isn't its native output
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): the terminal impedances ARE a Zout/Yout description. PSRR(f): not native (it models self-emission, not rejection of an injected upstream tone), though a 2-port version can. Noise PSD / spurs: this is its specialty — it carries both broadband emission and the discrete switching-harmonic comb as explicit sources with extracted impedance. PSS/HB: the extracted terminal network is small and (if passivity-enforced) HB-friendly; the deterministic source spectrum maps directly to injected HB tones. Most relevant as the template for 'how to attach a validated discrete-spur source behind an extracted Zout.'

methods.name: Supply-noise-to-jitter / sideband mapping: PSRR-based PSIJ and supply-induced phase modulation models
methods.what: Analytic/behavioral chain that converts a supply ripple/noise spectrum into output timing jitter or RF phase/amplitude sidebands using the block's PSRR(f) (or supply sensitivity Kvco / delay sensitivity). For drivers: PSIJ = supply ripple x PSRR shaped by edge slope and propagation delay; a 'jitter-aware target impedance' ties PDN Zpdn(f) to a jitter spec. For RF: a supply tone at fm modulates the carrier through PSRR/pushing, producing sidebands at fc +/- fm whose level = (carrier) x (PSRR/pushing at fm). The on-die LDO is analyzed by its PSRR alone and combined with the downstream block's sensitivity (modular: analyze LDO and load separately, then multiply/combine).
methods.where_used: High-speed link / clock / PLL / RF-frontend design. EMPSIJ (Efficient Modeling of Power-Supply-Induced Jitter), Missouri S&T PSIJ dissertations, IEEE EMC+SIPI papers on on-die LDO + output buffer, VCO supply-pushing literature; Cadence SpectreRF PSS+Pnoise/HB for sideband verification.
strengths: Directly produces the quantity the user cares about: sideband/spur level near a carrier from a supply tone via PSRR(f)
Modular (LDO PSRR x load sensitivity) -> few/fast simulations, generalizable across loads
Connects PDN Zout (which sets the ripple) to PSRR (which converts it) to the final sideband — full chain
Validatable against measured/simulated phase noise/spur tables
weaknesses: Linear-PSRR assumption: large supply swings cause AM/PM conversion and intermod not captured by a single PSRR(f)
Needs accurate PSRR phase (and pushing sign) for correct upper/lower sideband asymmetry
Edge-slope/delay approximations are driver-specific; RF-pushing models need the carrier circuit's actual sensitivity
Combining blocks by multiplication assumes weak interaction / no loading between LDO and load
methods.supply_handling: Zout(f): used upstream to compute the ripple amplitude (ripple = Zpdn x Iload). PSRR(f): the central operator — converts supply ripple to output sideband; phase critical. Noise PSD: broadband supply PSD x |PSRR|^2 -> output phase-noise skirt; spurs handled identically as discrete lines. Discrete spurs: explicitly the target — a supply spur at fm -> sideband at fc+/-fm at level set by PSRR(fm). PSS/HB: the recommended verification engine is exactly PSS/QPSS/HB + Pnoise/PAC: large tone = carrier, small tone = supply ripple; a lightweight LTI supply block (Zout+PSRR rational paths + injected spur sources) keeps the HB/QPSS problem small and convergent. This family is the most directly aligned with the project's end goal.

findings.relevance_to_spur_pss: Highly relevant, and it directly validates the project's current architecture. The PDN/PI community has independently converged on the same 'supply = impedance + noise + ripple' decomposition the project adopted: (1) a closed-loop Zout(f) 1-port for transient/ringing, (2) a PSRR(f) path that is THE mechanism turning a supply tone into carrier sidebands, (3) an output-referred (Norton-at-vout, loop-shaped) noise PSD, and (4) explicitly injected discrete spur tones (the EMI 3-terminal-Norton 'impedance + independent noise source' template). The key enabling technique for FAST, CONVERGENT PSS/HB is rational/pole-residue macromodeling (Vector Fitting + stability/causality — and for true immittances, passivity — enforcement) plus, for the surrounding passive network, Krylov MOR (PRIMA/SPRIM): both yield small, smooth, well-conditioned blocks that add no stiffness to the harmonic-balance Jacobian, unlike tabular-S convolution or switching PWL/POP models. Two critical, repeatedly-flagged caveats for spur fidelity: (a) PSRR is generally non-minimum-phase/sign-changing, so it must be fit from COMPLEX data and enforced only for stability/causality (NOT passivity) or the sideband phase/asymmetry will be wrong — this matches the project's V4 PSRR difficulty; (b) switching-converter PWL/SIMPLIS and average models belong OFFLINE (to extract Zout/PSRR/ripple-comb), not inside the GHz HB run, because of the MHz-vs-GHz timescale separation. For the actual carrier+ripple analysis, the literature points squarely at QPSS / PSS+PAC / HB+Pnoise with the regulator reduced to a low-order LTI 2-port plus injected tones — exactly the lightweight, linear-multiport model the project found sufficient (spurs <-62 dBc, nonlinearity a red herring).

methods.name: Passive rational 2-port PSRR=S21 + Zout=S22 (vector-fit Verilog-A) with LPTV PAC/PXF/Pnoise spur translation; op-point parameterization and TVP as extensions
methods.what: Recommended composite. Fit supply-to-output PSRR(f) and output impedance Zout(f) to a passive pole-residue rational form via Vector Fitting, realize in SPICE/Verilog-A, add shaped-Norton output noise and forced spur tones. Spurs near a carrier come from LPTV PAC/PXF/Pnoise folding a supply ripple at df to sidebands at k*f0+/-df with phase. Extensions: parameterize Zout/PSRR/poles by load current (X-params/PHD or interpolated fits); TVP compresses the LPTV system to a compact LTI+mixer. Guran 2022 cut PSRR error to 0-7 percent below 500 kHz.
methods.where_used: Behavioral LDO/PMIC models (ROHM/TI/ADI); Cadence Spectre/SpectreRF (PSS/HB+pac/pxf/pnoise), ADS (HB, X-params), AFS; vector fitting SINTEF/scikit-rf/IMEC; ADI/TI spur-threshold bench; TVP academic.
strengths: PSRR/Zout phase-correct, dominant near-carrier spur quantities
passive rational form HB-friendly; passivity is the key convergence lever
PAC/PXF exact ripple-to-sideband with phase; Pnoise cyclostationary noise
X-params add load dependence HB-natively; TVP gives a compact sideband model
weaknesses: base 2-port linear about one op point (misses gm compression/slew/dropout)
discrete spurs must be injected as forced tones
harmonic-count K aliases spurs; large interferers need QPAC/multitone HB
LUT/ML op-point models stall HB unless smoothed; ML non-passive
methods.supply_handling: Zout/PSRR as fitted S22/S21 transfers; noise shaped-Norton+PSRR; spurs forced at vout. PSS/HB supply-to-sideband is exact via the conversion matrix (inject at df, appears at k*f0+/-df with phase); Pnoise folds noise PSD to carrier. Passivity/poles and K govern convergence/accuracy; separated supply-tone-vs-carrier favors envelope/multitone HB or QPAC over plain shooting.
