synthesis.landscape_summary:
The mainstream LDO/supply-modeling landscape splits into four layers that our project touches. (1) Analytical/classical small-signal theory — Rincon-Mora's impedance-divider PSRR (PSRdc=(Aolβ)^-1, zero at BWA, pole at UGF, output pole, ESR zero) and the feedback identity Zout(s)=Zo,ol/(1+T(s)) that explains the inductive +20dB/dec rise and the load-step ring. This is a recipe, not a runnable model; you realize its extracted poles/zeros as a behavioral block. (2) Behavioral macromodeling in Verilog-A/SPICE — either controlled-source equivalent circuits (Boyle/Renesas G-R-L-E ladders) or laplace_nd transfer-function blocks, with a hard Kundert constraint that SpectreRF supports only ddt/idt/idtmod/Laplace state (delay, z-transform, transition/slew/cross, random are hidden-state and either abort or silently kill sideband propagation). (3) Black-box rational/passive macromodeling from the SI/PI world — Vector Fitting (Gustavsen-Semlyen, relaxed/orthonormal variants), Sanathanan-Koerner, and the newer AAA and Loewner fitters, followed by stability/passivity enforcement (Hamiltonian/half-size tests) and RLC+controlled-source synthesis, plus delay-extraction/DEPACT for non-minimum-phase responses. (4) Nonlinear HB-native frequency-domain models — X-parameters/PHD and hot-S (the 6=4+2 / S+conjugate-T result), whose conceptual core (a small supply tone at fc±fd scatters into coupled upper/lower sidebands needing both direct and conjugate terms) is exactly what Cadence PAC/PXF compute and what governs spur amplitude AND phase asymmetry.

The PDN/power-integrity community has independently converged on precisely the abstraction our project uses: model the regulator as Zout(f) (1-port for transient/ringing/anti-resonance) plus a PSRR(f) path (the operator that turns supply ripple into carrier sidebands) plus an output-referred noise source plus explicitly injected discrete spur tones — the EMI '3-terminal Norton: impedance + independent source' template. The recommended composite from multiple survey agents is verbatim our architecture: passive rational 2-port (PSRR≈S21, Zout≈S22) + shaped-Norton noise + forced spur tones, then PSS/HB with PAC/PXF/Pnoise to fold ripple/noise to sidebands. Op-point parameterization by load current and the finding that the near-carrier spur band is linear (so a linear multiport plus injected tones suffices) are both endorsed.

Where our project genuinely departs from the mainstream is the hard ban on laplace_nd / s-domain behavioral blocks in favor of pure lumped R/L/C + controlled sources. The survey is split here: the Verilog-A/Kundert camp considers laplace_nd the *best-conditioned* PSS/HB-legal primitive (Beckett: a hand-rolled high-order ddt ODE fails at min timestep where laplace_nd converges), while the SI/PI synthesis camp agrees that a hand-built RLC+controlled-source subckt is the *safest* representation for PSS/HB and notes some simulators evaluate laplace blocks differently in HB vs transient, hurting spur fidelity. So our choice is defensible but is a deliberate trade of fitting expressiveness (especially complex-conjugate and RHP-zero/delay phase) for guaranteed convergence and portability.

The one place our implementation is provably behind the state of the art is PSRR phase on non-minimum-phase architectures. Our PSRR is realized as i_c(s)=G0+Σ Gᵢ/(1+s/wᵢ) — a bank of signed *first-order, strictly-real-pole* sections (the SK fitter returns None and falls back to a min-phase shelf whenever poles come out complex), multiplied by the same Zout. Signed real sections give sign reversals and magnitude notches but bounded phase; they structurally cannot reproduce the phase of a genuine RHP zero or net loop delay. That is exactly our documented residual: V4 magnitude fits to 0.04 dB but phase is 25° off (the '390° phase race'), and V3's migrating multi-pole leaves a 10° phase residual. The survey points straight at the fixes we have not yet adopted: signed second-order (complex-conjugate RLC) sections, explicit delay extraction (e^-sτ, exact per-harmonic in HB), and AAA/Loewner/delayed-VF fitters that make no minimum-phase assumption.

comparison_rows.dimension: Core supply abstraction (what the model IS)
comparison_rows.mainstream: PDN/PI standard + survey 'recommended composite': passive rational 2-port Zout≈S22 + PSRR≈S21 + output-referred (shaped-Norton) noise + injected discrete spur tones; spurs folded to sidebands by PSS/HB + PAC/PXF/Pnoise.
comparison_rows.ours: Exactly this: per-block Zout + signed-section PSRR + decoupled Norton-@vout noise + deterministic SIN spur tones at vout, with a PSS/HB fundamental manifest.
comparison_rows.assessment: Dead even — we independently landed on the textbook-endorsed architecture. No advantage either way; this is the right structure for the spur goal.

comparison_rows.mainstream: Two accepted routes: (a) laplace_nd Verilog-A (Kundert/Beckett: best-conditioned, PSS/HB-legal, supports complex poles + non-min-phase fits + explicit delay); (b) synthesized RLC + controlled sources from a pole-residue fit (safest, simulator-agnostic).
comparison_rows.ours: Route (b) ONLY, by hard constraint: pure R/L/C + VCVS/VCCS/B/SIN, no laplace_nd ever.
comparison_rows.assessment: Mixed. We get maximal convergence robustness and portability (ngspice→Spectre) and avoid the HB-vs-transient laplace evaluation discrepancy. But we forfeit laplace_nd's easy complex-pole and delay realization — which is the exact tool the mainstream uses for the non-min-phase PSRR phase we are stuck on.

comparison_rows.mainstream: Zout=Zo,ol/(1+T) realized as low-order rational/RLC; complex-pole pair for the resonant peak; VF + passivity enforcement on the (genuinely passive) Zout port; Krylov MOR (PRIMA/SPRIM) for the surrounding passive PDN.
comparison_rows.ours: (R_a+sL_a)||(ESR+1/sCout) with optional 2nd R-L branch; multi-start bounded LS; Cout auto-extracted; quadratic-in-ln(iload) op-parameterization.
comparison_rows.assessment: Close, with two gaps. We are inherently passive-by-construction (good, matches the 'enforce passivity only on Zout' rule for free). But (i) we do not explicitly enforce/verify passivity via Hamiltonian tests, and (ii) our fixed 1-2 branch topology under-fits load-migrating multi-pole resonance (V3 pkf residual) where a free-order VF/AAA fit would do better.

comparison_rows.mainstream: Fit from COMPLEX data with stability-only (NOT passivity) enforcement; use signed 2nd-order sections, RHP zeros explicit (SK/AAA/Loewner make no min-phase assumption), or delay extraction (e^-sτ, exact per harmonic in HB) for net loop delay.
comparison_rows.ours: SK-fit i_c=H/Zout but realized as STRICTLY-REAL first-order signed sections (NPS=3); falls back to min-phase shelf if poles are complex. Magnitude excellent (V4 0.04 dB); phase bounded → 25° (V4) / 10° (V3) residual.
comparison_rows.assessment: Mainstream is clearly ahead here, and this is our dominant residual. Real-pole-only sections cannot carry RHP-zero/delay phase. We correctly use SK (the survey notes SK is arguably cleaner than VF for explicit RHP zeros) but then discard its complex/RHP content at realization.

comparison_rows.mainstream: Output-referred noise = referred sources × noise gain, but loop/Zout-shaped; survey confirms the correct generalization is a shaped Norton at vout (In=Sv/|Zout|) feeding Pnoise/Hbnoise for cyclostationary sideband folding.
comparison_rows.ours: Decoupled Norton-@vout: In=Sv_meas/|Zout|, white + 6 Lorentzians fit jointly in log-domain across 3 corners, realized as white-R + R||C sections + VCCS. Explicitly decoupled so it survives Zout topology changes.
comparison_rows.assessment: We are at or ahead of mainstream. The decoupling fix (so adding a Zout branch doesn't re-shape noise) is a genuinely good engineering refinement that the generic literature does not emphasize. Essentially closed (npsd ≤ ~2-3.6 dB).

comparison_rows.mainstream: LTI core never creates tones; inject deterministic periodic sources at vout; PSS/HB folds them to fc±m·fspur. Classical small-signal cannot predict them; NMOS-SF charge-pump is a flagged self-spur source.
comparison_rows.ours: Deterministic SIN current tones at vout (I_k=vout_amp_k/|Zout(f_k)|), fundamentals only, transient-FFT characterized, with a GCD-fold commensurate/incommensurate PSS fundamental manifest; supply-coupled spurs left to ride the PSRR path from vin.
comparison_rows.assessment: We are ahead in execution. The fundamentals-only + GCD manifest + 'inject aggressor at vin, not vout' discipline is more rigorous than the generic 'add a tone' advice and correctly respects that downstream nonlinearity regenerates IM products. Closed.

comparison_rows.mainstream: Parameterize fits by load (interpolated fits, or bias/load-dependent X-parameters); smoothness across the axis is a documented HB-convergence liability.
comparison_rows.ours: Quadratic in ln(iload) through 3 corners {20,121,250µA} with envelope clamping to prevent off-corner overshoot.
comparison_rows.assessment: Aligned and pragmatic. 3-corner quadratic is lighter than X-param tables and the clamp directly addresses the smoothness/overshoot risk the survey flags. Adequate given the linear near-carrier regime; X-param-grade only needed if large-signal/load-migration coupling matters.

comparison_rows.mainstream: Vector Fitting is the de-facto SI/PI standard (relaxed/orthonormal); AAA and Loewner are emerging, parameter-free, auto-order, no min-phase assumption, better at sharp notches/high-Q with few poles; SK for explicit num/den.
comparison_rows.ours: Bespoke: multi-start bounded LS for Zout (fixed topology), SK iteration for PSRR, log-domain joint LS for noise.
comparison_rows.assessment: Mainstream tooling is more capable for the hard cases. Our fixed-topology Zout LS under-fits migrating resonances; AAA/Loewner would auto-select order and capture them. Our SK is appropriate but under-exploited (real-pole-only). This is the biggest adoptable-tooling gap.

comparison_rows.mainstream: Keep linear-dominant, C∞, every node touches a linear element (Brinson); enforce passivity on Zout; beware shooting-Pnoise under-reporting LF supply-noise upconversion at slow supply nodes.
comparison_rows.ours: All-lumped-passive-by-construction guarantees this; no internal regulation loop to stall; inherently smooth.
comparison_rows.assessment: We are ahead on robustness — our constraint buys exactly the property the survey says is the dominant convergence lever, for free, and across engines (validated in ngspice). The cost is purely expressiveness, paid in the PSRR phase gap.

comparison_rows.mainstream: Typically AC for Zout/PSRR + Pnoise for noise + PSS/PAC for spurs, often per single part; survey notes spur work needs complex (mag+phase) data and multi-analysis grading.
comparison_rows.ours: Unified weighted composite over Zout/PSRR mag+phase, transient load-step, noise PSD, spur match, across ~14 DUT variants; coherent-window transient-FFT for spurs (not .noise).
comparison_rows.assessment: Ahead. A multi-variant, multi-analysis, phase-aware composite scoring harness is more disciplined than typical published practice and is what lets us even see the V1/V3/V4 phase residuals quantitatively.

where_we_align: Core 2-port architecture (Zout S22 + PSRR S21 + shaped-Norton noise + injected spur tones) is verbatim the survey's 'recommended composite' and the PDN/PI community's independently-derived 'supply = impedance + noise + ripple' abstraction.

Output-referred shaped Norton noise In=Sv/|Zout| is exactly the loop-shaped generalization the noise literature (TI SLAA412/ADI AN-1120 corrected for Zout shaping) points to, feeding Pnoise sideband folding.
Spurs as deterministic injected tones (LTI core never self-generates) matches the universal finding that rational/linear macromodels only relay tones; spurs must be explicit sources.

Linear-multiport-suffices for the near-carrier spur band matches both our own measurement (<-62 dBc harmonics) and the survey consensus that the spur band is small-signal/linear, so X-params/Volterra are unnecessary and risk non-passive in-loop behavior.
Using Sanathanan-Koerner for PSRR identification is endorsed — the survey explicitly notes SK is arguably cleaner than VF when you want RHP zeros explicit in the numerator.
Passive-by-construction Zout aligns with the rule 'enforce passivity on Zout, never on the active PSRR path' — we get the passive-Zout property structurally and we keep PSRR a free (signed) transfer.
All-lumped R/L/C + controlled-source synthesis from a pole-residue model is one of the two mainstream-endorsed realizations and is the one the SI/PI camp calls the SAFEST for PSS/HB.

Op-point parameterization by load current with anti-overshoot (envelope) clamping matches the parameterized-fit approach while directly mitigating the table-smoothness convergence liability the survey flags.
Keeping noise as PSD sources and spurs as separate injected tones (out of the large-signal balance) matches the Brinson/Kundert HB discipline for behavioral blocks.
where_we_differ_for_better: Hard ban on laplace_nd in favor of pure lumped R/L/C + controlled sources: deliberately trades fit expressiveness for guaranteed cross-engine PSS/HB/shooting robustness and portability (ngspice ground-truth → Spectre target). Justified because the survey names passivity/smoothness/linear-element-per-node as the DOMINANT convergence lever, and flags that some simulators evaluate laplace blocks differently in HB vs transient (a direct spur-fidelity risk) — our constraint sidesteps that entirely.

Decoupling the noise Norton from the Zout synthesis (In=Sv/|Zout| with an independent realized network) so adding a 2nd Zout branch does not re-shape the noise — a refinement beyond generic 'shaped Norton' advice; it fixed our V1/V3 noise regression (9.6/21.8→1.0/2.1 dB) when branch B was engaged for Zout.

Fundamentals-only spur emission with a GCD-fold PSS fundamental manifest and the 'inject the aggressor at vin, let PSRR carry it' rule: more rigorous than 'add a tone at the output' because it (a) avoids double-counting IM products the user's downstream nonlinearity regenerates, and (b) keeps supply-coupled spurs on the physically-correct PSRR path with correct commensurate/incommensurate fundamental bookkeeping for PSS.

Data-driven per-block selector (auto-choose shelf-vs-bank PSRR, 1-vs-2 Zout branches, spur on/off) rather than one monolithic model — gives zero regression on the 9 min-phase variants while engaging complexity only where data demands it, which is better generalization discipline than a fixed high-order template.

Unified phase-aware composite scoring across ~14 architectural variants and all analyses, rather than per-part magnitude-only validation — this is why we can even quantify the V4 25°/V3 10° phase residuals that lesser harnesses would miss.
where_we_lag: PSRR phase on non-minimum-phase architectures is our dominant unresolved residual and the place mainstream is clearly ahead. Our i_c=G0+ΣGᵢ/(1+s/wᵢ) uses STRICTLY-REAL first-order sections (the SK fitter returns None and reverts to a min-phase shelf the moment poles come out complex). Signed real sections give magnitude notches/sign flips but bounded phase — they structurally cannot carry the phase of a genuine RHP zero or net loop delay. Result: V4 magnitude 0.04 dB but phase 25° off; V3 phase 10° off. laplace_nd or signed 2nd-order/delay realizations would close this.

No complex-conjugate (2nd-order RLC) PSRR sections at all — we only have them on the Zout path. The survey's capless/multi-stage and delay-extraction literature treats complex-pole pairs as essential for resonant/non-min-phase PSRR.

No explicit transport-delay (e^-sτ) modeling. The survey's delay-extraction/DEPACT/delayed-VF family represents net loop delay with low rational order, and in HB a pure delay is an EXACT per-harmonic phase term — the most direct fix for our 390° 'phase race' that real poles cannot reach.

Fixed-topology Zout (1-2 R-L branches) under-fits load-migrating multi-pole resonance (V3 resonance migrates 0.27→10 MHz with load). Free-order VF/AAA/Loewner fitters auto-select the order and capture migrating/high-Q resonances with fewer artifacts.

We do not run an explicit passivity verification/enforcement step (Hamiltonian/half-size test). We are passive-by-construction on Zout so this is low-risk, but we have no automated guard against an accidentally non-passive synthesized network, and no formal stability-only check on the active PSRR path.

We do not leverage the standard, more powerful fitters (relaxed/orthonormal VF, AAA, Loewner) that the SI/PI world considers baseline — our bespoke LS/SK is adequate for the easy variants but is the limiting factor on the hard ones.

No use of the hot-S / X_S-X_T (S + conjugate-T) insight to explicitly check upper/lower sideband ASYMMETRY. We rely on PSS/PAC to fold our injected tones; correct sideband asymmetry depends entirely on getting PSRR/Zout phase right, which is exactly our weak spot.
adoptable_ideas: For Target B AND the Zout/PSRR fidelity task: adopt a real Vector Fitting / AAA fitter (scikit-rf VectorFitting or AAA) as the front-end for Zout and PSRR instead of fixed-topology LS. Auto-order selection directly addresses V3's migrating multi-pole resonance and gives a principled pole count; then synthesize to RLC+controlled-source as we already do. AAA/Loewner additionally make NO minimum-phase assumption, so they capture RHP-zero/notch PSRR magnitude+phase the real-pole bank drops.

HIGHEST PRIORITY for V4/V3 phase: add signed SECOND-ORDER (complex-conjugate-pole) sections to the PSRR coupling current, realized as RLC + VCCS (no laplace_nd needed — a damped 2nd-order section is a passive RLC driving a VCCS). This lifts the phase ceiling that strictly-real sections impose. Stop discarding the SK fit's complex poles; realize them as RLC sections.

For the V4 '390° phase race' specifically: do explicit delay extraction H(s)=e^-sτ·H_rational(s) (fit τ by Brent/iterative, Hilbert-causality-checked), then realize the rational part as we do and the e^-sτ as a low-order Bessel/Padé all-pass made of R/L/C + controlled sources (stays within our lumped constraint). In the eventual Spectre HB run the delay is an exact per-harmonic phase term — the cheapest path to correct sideband phase asymmetry.

Fit PSRR from COMPLEX (real+imag) data with STABILITY-ONLY enforcement, never passivity (PSRR<0 dB / gain is legitimately non-passive). We already fit complex but should add an explicit RHP-pole flip + stability guard and a check that we are not magnitude-only anywhere.

Add an automated passivity verification (Hamiltonian / half-size singularity test, available in scikit-rf) on the synthesized Zout port as a CI gate in the scoring harness — cheap insurance that a refit never emits a non-passive Zout that would stall Spectre HB.

Use the hot-S / S+conjugate-T (6=4+2) lens as a validation metric: after PSS+PAC on the emitted model, explicitly compare upper vs lower sideband (fc±fd) amplitude AND phase against the transistor-level reference. This turns our PSRR-phase residual into a directly spur-relevant pass/fail and tells us when phase error actually matters for asymmetry.

For Target B (real Cadence LDO import): mirror the EMI 3-terminal-Norton extraction workflow — extract Zout(f) and PSRR(f) by AC (shunt-through for Zout), the noise PSD by Pnoise, and the spur comb by coherent transient-FFT, then run the same VF/AAA→RLC pipeline. Beware the documented SpectreRF shooting-Pnoise caveat: a slow LDO supply pole can cause LF supply-noise upconversion to be UNDER-reported — validate noise against HB-Pnoise or a fine PAC sweep, not shooting alone.

For Target B convergence: keep switching/PWL or SIMPLIS-POP characterization strictly OFFLINE (MHz fsw vs GHz carrier timescale separation makes it unusable inside the HB run); extract Zout/PSRR/ripple-comb from it, then inject as our rational paths + forced tones.

Consider relaxed Vector Fitting specifically for noisy real-silicon Target-B data — its improved pole relocation handles measurement noise far better than naive SK over the mHz-to-100MHz LDO span where monomial bases are ill-conditioned.
synthesis.verdict: Our architecture is correct and, in several respects (noise decoupling, spur manifest/aggressor-at-vin discipline, multi-variant phase-aware scoring, convergence-by-construction), ahead of generic published practice — the survey's 'recommended composite' is essentially a description of what we built. The genuine, well-justified departure is banning laplace_nd for pure lumped R/L/C: it buys best-in-class PSS/HB robustness and cross-engine portability at the cost of fit expressiveness. That cost is now concentrated in exactly one place: PSRR (and migrating-Zout) PHASE on non-minimum-phase architectures, because our PSRR coupling current is restricted to strictly-real first-order sections that have a bounded phase ceiling. This is not a reinvention problem (we are not duplicating a standard we ignored) but an under-exploitation problem (we use SK then throw away its complex/RHP content at realization). The single highest-leverage next move is to add signed second-order (complex-conjugate RLC) PSRR sections plus, for the V4 phase race, explicit delay extraction realized as a lumped all-pass — both stay inside the no-laplace constraint — and to swap the fixed-topology Zout LS for a real VF/AAA fitter with auto-order and a Hamiltonian passivity gate. For Target B, reuse this same extract→VF/AAA→RLC pipeline, keep switching characterization offline, validate sideband asymmetry with the hot-S (S+conjugate-T) lens, and watch the shooting-Pnoise LF-upconversion caveat.
